Sake poured here comes direct from the Sekiya Brewery, with staff offering food-pairing guidance based on the character of each bottle.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 公式サイト・IDENTITY名古屋によると、四間道・堀川西岸の築約150年の米蔵（江戸期）を改装した店。三河の蔵元・関谷醸造（蓬莱泉・空）の直営。理念は『和醸良酒』。土壁と柱を残す。レストラン32席・バー16席。

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information about cross-contamination controls is published. Tempura is offered on the à la carte menu, indicating wheat flour is actively used in the kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Wheat-containing soy sauce appears throughout the menu: in simmered dishes (鶏肉の醤油煮), dressings (醤油麹ドレッシング), grilled items, and course meals. No tamari or GF soy sauce alternatives are mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen prepares meat and seafood across all courses. The vegan course is a separate reservation-only offering, but no information about dedicated preparation areas or cross-contact prevention is provided.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Reservations are phone-only; staff can be informed of dietary needs at time of booking. The course menu notes that nut allergies (walnuts) should be disclosed in advance, suggesting some allergy awareness, but no formal allergy protocol is described.

positives.text: A dedicated vegan course is available on request with 3 days advance notice, indicating the kitchen has the capability to prepare plant-based meals when given adequate preparation time.

cultural_comfort.note: Staff speak limited English and the menu is Japanese-only; some staff have made efforts to help non-Japanese speakers, though experiences vary — a Japanese-speaking companion or translation app is strongly recommended.

places.name: レストラン角家 (Restaurant Kadoya)
places.gf_detail: This is a tonkatsu and set-meal diner: breaded cutlets (wheat panko), wheat-based sauces, and standard soy sauce (contains wheat) are core to the menu. No gluten-free options have been noted anywhere. Cross-contamination from frying is highly likely given shared fryer use for tonkatsu and kara-age. Not suitable for celiac diners; those with mild gluten sensitivity should discuss options in Japanese with the kitchen before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is almost entirely meat-centric (tonkatsu, kara-age, hamburg, pork loin, omurice with chicken rice). No dedicated vegetarian or vegan dishes are listed. Standard Japanese dashi (likely bonito-based) almost certainly underpins sauces and soups. No vegan accommodation has been reported by any reviewer; a Japanese-speaker should inquire before visiting.
places.neighborhood: 西区児玉 / 浄心 (Joshin)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A long-running family-operated shokudo tucked into a residential block a few minutes' walk from Joshin Station in Nagoya's Nishi Ward. Three generations work the restaurant together: the husband and wife team the kitchen, their daughter manages the dining room, and the grandmothe

chef_bio.background: A long-running family-operated shokudo tucked into a residential block near Joshin Station in Nagoya's Nishi Ward. Three generations work the restaurant together: the husband and wife team the kitchen, their daughter manages the dining room, and the grandmother lends a hand — a setup that has made it a genuine neighbourhood institution.
chef_bio.philosophy: Generous portions, honest prices, and the kind of cooking that keeps a neighbourhood fed decade after decade. The menu runs from teishoku through tonkatsu, hamburger steak, kara-age, curry and noodles — a deliberate breadth so that every member of a family or work group finds something.
chef_bio.specialty: Omurice — an oversized rugby-ball-shaped omelette rice (¥650), finished with chūnō (Japanese brown) sauce rather than ketchup, that regulars travel across the city to order. The large version is memorably enormous.

anecdotes.text: Food bloggers describe the omurice large as 'rugby-ball-shaped,' an unusually striking presentation for a humble neighbourhood diner.
One blogger noted that sharing two or three dishes between diners is the practical approach, as every dish arrives at a scale that 'approaches large-eating territory.'
The warm orange-lit dining room with wall-mounted yellow menu cards and a tatami back room gives it the feel of a place unchanged for decades — a quality regulars cite as part of its appeal.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 名古屋めし等によると、浄心駅近く西区の3世代家族経営の食堂（厨房は夫婦、ホールは娘、祖母が補助）。看板はラグビーボール大のオムライス（中濃ソース仕上げ、650円）。とんかつ・ハンバーグ・カレー等メニュー豊富。オレンジ色の店内に黄色いメニュー札、奥に座敷。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tonkatsu (breaded pork cutlet) and kara-age (fried chicken) are core menu items; both are wheat-breaded or wheat-battered, and no dedicated fryer for gluten-free items has been reported. Shared-fryer cross-contamination is the expected default.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce (含む小麦) is the assumed condiment throughout the menu; no tamari or wheat-free soy sauce alternative has been mentioned by any source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All set meals include meat or seafood as the protein; kitchen surfaces and cookware are shared. No vegan-only preparation zone or protocol has been mentioned anywhere.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy handling protocol has been documented by any reviewer or the restaurant itself. This is a small family-run kitchen; allergen queries would need to be made in Japanese directly with the family.

positives.text: Family-run kitchen with direct communication possible; a Japanese-speaking diner can speak face-to-face with the people actually cooking the food, which is preferable to large chain restaurants where kitchen staff may be untrained on allergy protocols.

cultural_comfort.note: A neighborhood shokudo with no English menu or signage; ordering requires basic Japanese or pointing at the wall-mounted menu cards, but the family atmosphere is welcoming and unhurried.

places.name: motsu・sen（もつせん） (Motsu Sen)
places.gf_detail: Motsu-suki uses sukiyaki-style sauce containing wheat-based soy sauce. The signature champon noodle finish contains wheat. Motsu nabe broth typically also relies on soy sauce. No gluten-free options or allergen accommodations are mentioned on any source. Celiacs should not eat here.
places.vegan_detail: This is a dedicated offal (motsu) specialty restaurant. Every course dish — from raw heart and tripe sashimi to the signature motsu-suki and motsu nabe — is meat-based. There are no plant-based mains or noted vegan substitutions.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Thu & Sun 17:30–23:00 (food L.O. 22:00, drink L.O. 22:30), Fri/Sat 17:30–24:00 (food L.O. 23:00, drink L.O. 23:30)
places.neighborhood: 国際センター / 円頓寺横丁 (Entdon-ji Yokocho), Nishi Ward
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened in May 2023 in the narrow alley of Entdon-ji Yokocho (円頓寺横丁) in Nishi Ward, motsu・sen was produced in partnership with a well-known hormone yakiniku restaurant from Shibuya, Tokyo. The concept brought a refined, date-night sensibility to offal cuisine — a stone-and-wood in

chef_bio.background: Opened in May 2023 in the narrow alley of Entdon-ji Yokocho (円頓寺横丁) in Nishi Ward, motsu・sen was produced in partnership with a well-known hormone (offal) yakiniku restaurant from Shibuya, Tokyo. The concept brought a refined, date-night sensibility to offal cuisine — a stone-and-wood interior hung with work by contemporary artist Miura Koga.
chef_bio.philosophy: Fresh offal enjoyed daily — the owner emphasises that offal must be savoured at peak freshness, and personally oversees sourcing and seasoning details including the Cambodian pepper used to finish dishes.
chef_bio.specialty: もつすき (motsu-suki) — a sukiyaki-style preparation of fresh offal that the shop bills as a Nagoya rarity. Five varieties of fresh motsu are used; the broth is ignited tableside, then transforms into a full motsu nabe. The meal finishes with champon noodles dusted with Cambodian pepper sourced personally by the owner.

anecdotes.text: The owner personally developed the use of Cambodian pepper as the finishing spice for the champon noodles, a signature flavour detail that sets the dish apart.
A limited Ladies' Course (Nov 2023–Mar 2024) at ¥3,630 was introduced to attract female diners, including a white-sesame panna cotta dessert — unusual for an offal restaurant.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 名古屋ドットジェイピー等によると、2023年5月、西区・円頓寺横丁にオープン。渋谷の有名ホルモン焼肉店プロデュース、店内には三浦コーガの作品。5種のもつを使う『もつすき』は卓上で炊き上げて鍋へ、締めはカンボジア産胡椒のちゃんぽん。2023年11月〜2024年3月にレディースコース3,630円（胡麻のパンナコッタ付）。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Champon noodles (wheat) are the standard course finale; the kitchen handles wheat throughout service.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Motsu-suki uses sukiyaki-style sauce, which traditionally and in this context is wheat-based soy sauce. No tamari or GF soy sauce alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Every dish in every course is meat or offal-based; there is no plant-only preparation zone or vegan option.

cultural_comfort.note: Small 16-seat counter-and-table izakaya in a yokocho alley; staff are likely Japanese-only speakers, and the menu and ordering are conducted in Japanese with no noted English support.

places.name: ルーキーズこだわり生パスタ。時々ふんわりパンケーキ。 (Rookies Kodawari Nama Pasta. Tokidoki Funwar)
places.gf_detail: As the shop name proclaims "fresh pasta," fresh noodles made primarily from wheat flour are the mainstay product. There is no mention in any source of a gluten-free menu or wheat-free options. Both the pasta and pancakes are prepared in an environment that uses large amounts of wheat, so it is judged not accommodating for people with celiac disease or wheat allergy.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on fresh pasta and pancakes. Dishes are mainly made with dairy and eggs, and no vegan menu has been confirmed on either the official or external sites. Mentaiko cream pasta, Napolitan, and avocado cheese toast are the main items.
places.hours_raw: Sat, Sun 10:30–16:00. Closed Mon–Fri. Irregular closures during events. Business days in February and March should be confirmed via the calendar, etc.
places.neighborhood: Ninomaru, Naka Ward (Aichi Prefectural Gymnasium 1F) / 5 min walk from Nagoyajō Station
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A weekend-only casual cafe that opened on September 28, 2022, on the first floor of the Aichi Prefectural Gymnasium. Taking advantage of its location within a public facility in the Nagoya Castle area, it targets tourists and local families as its main clientele.

chef_bio.background: 2022年9月28日、愛知県体育館1階にオープンした週末営業のカジュアルカフェ。名古屋城エリアの公共施設内という立地を活かし、観光客や地元ファミリー層を主要客層としている。
chef_bio.philosophy: 手打ちの生麺にこだわったパスタを、体育館という日常的な空間で気軽に楽しんでもらうことを志向している。
chef_bio.specialty: こだわりの生パスタ（明太子クリーム紫蘇パスタ、昔ながらの鉄板ナポリタンなど）とふんわりパンケーキ。

anecdotes.text: 愛知県体育館の1Fという珍しいロケーションで、キッズスペースを完備しファミリー層に人気。名古屋城エリアの環境が特徴とされる。

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: ホットペッパー等によると、2022年9月28日、愛知県体育館1階にオープンした土日営業のカフェ。名古屋城エリア、キッズスペース完備でファミリー向け。明太子クリーム紫蘇パスタや鉄板ナポリタン等の生パスタとふんわりパンケーキが看板。

gf_cross_contamination.text: 生パスタが主力商品であり、厨房内に小麦粉が常時大量に存在する環境。グルテンフリー対応に関する情報はどのソースにも記載がなく、交差汚染リスクは高いと推定される。

vegan_cross_contact.text: 乳製品・卵を使った料理が主力で、ヴィーガン向けメニューの設定はなく、動物性食材との接触を回避する体制の記載は確認されていない。

cultural_comfort.note: 愛知県体育館内のカジュアルなカフェで、スタッフとのやり取りは日本語が基本。英語対応は確認されていない。

places.name: 名古屋名物 みそかつ 矢場とん 名古屋城 金シャチ横丁店 (Nagoya Meibutsu Misokatsu Yabaton Nagoya Cas)
places.gf_detail: Miso katsu is fundamentally incompatible with a gluten-free diet: every cutlet is dredged in wheat flour and then coated in panko (wheat-based breadcrumbs) before frying. The restaurant operates a single shared fryer environment with no documented gluten-free protocol. The retail miso sauce product lists soy, pork, and rice as allergens (wheat not separately declared), but the in-restaurant coating is the primary and unavoidable wheat source. No GF options exist on the menu, and no allergen-accommodation procedures are published. Celiac diners cannot eat safely here.
places.vegan_detail: The entire menu centres on deep-fried pork cutlets (tonkatsu) coated in panko and served with Yabaton's signature red-miso pork-based sauce. No vegetarian or vegan dishes are offered. Side items include shredded cabbage and miso soup, but the kitchen handles pork throughout.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sun, day before holidays & holidays 10:30–17:30 (L.O. 17:00)
places.neighborhood: Naka-ku, Sannomaru / Kinshachi Yokochō (Yoshinao Zone)
places.notes: Yabaton was founded in 1947 by Suzuki Yoshio as a small stall called 「矢場のとんかつ」 (Yaba no Tonkatsu) near the Minami-Ohtsu-dori 4-chome streetcar stop in Nagoya. The name derives from the Yabachō neighbourhood — the area now known as Ōsu — where the original stall stood. By 1953 the

chef_bio.origin: 名古屋市（矢場町）、1947年創業
chef_bio.background: Yabaton was founded in 1947 by Suzuki Yoshio (鈴木義夫) as a small stall called 「矢場のとんかつ」 (Yaba no Tonkatsu) near the Minami-Ōtsu-dori 4-chome streetcar stop in Nagoya. The name derives from the Yaba (矢場, old archery-range) area near today's Ōsu district, where the original stall stood. The business was incorporated in 1953, and the flagship Yabachō restaurant was established in 1970 under the full name 名古屋名物みそかつ矢場とん. The chain has since grown to around 30 locations across Japan and expanded internationally (Thailand in 2014, later Taiwan), while maintaining a family-run ethos.
chef_bio.philosophy: Yabaton positions itself as 「一流の大衆食堂」 — a first-class people's diner. The kitchen ages its red-miso across the seasons to produce a sauce richer and more aromatic than commercially brewed miso. The original spirit of the dish — an accidental, joyful discovery — informs the restaurant's unpretentious, generous style.
chef_bio.specialty: みそかつ — deep-fried pork cutlet (loin or fillet) topped with a rich, naturally aged Nagoya red-miso (豆味噌) sauce simmered for hours. The signature item is the わらじとんかつ (waraji tonkatsu), an oversized loin cutlet shaped like a traditional straw sandal.

anecdotes.text: Miso katsu was born by accident: Suzuki Yoshio, watching a customer at his stall dip skewered pork cutlets into a pot of dote-nabe (Nagoya-style miso offal broth), tasted the combination and, according to Yabaton's own account, found it delicious — then spent years perfecting a standalone miso sauce to recreate that moment.
The restaurant kept a 27-year continuous presence at Nagoya Stadium from 1972 to 1996, making it a fixture of match-day culture for a generation of Nagoyans.

The Kinshachi Yokocho branch sits in the Yoshinao Zone — the traditionalist half of the food street flanking Nagoya Castle's main gate — chosen to reflect the castle's historical character rather than the livelier Muneharu Zone.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 矢場とん公式沿革によると、1947年に鈴木義夫が『矢場のとんかつ』として創業（南大津通四丁目電停付近、矢場＝現・大須近辺が名の由来）。1953年法人化、1970年に矢場町本店を開設し『矢場とん』に。全国約30店＋タイ（2014）・台湾へ展開。味噌かつはどて鍋にとんかつを浸した客をヒントに考案。1972〜96年ナゴヤ球場に27年間出店。金シャチ横丁店は義直ゾーン。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Every item on the menu is deep-fried in a shared fryer environment. There is no documentation of a dedicated gluten-free fryer, a gluten-free menu section, or any separation protocol for wheat-sensitive guests.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The miso sauce is the centrepiece of the dish. The retail version of the sauce lists soy (大豆), pork, and rice as declared allergens without separately listing wheat; however, Nagoya red-miso (豆味噌/八丁味噌) is traditionally made from soybeans only and does not inherently contain wheat — but the restaurant's full preparation environment cannot be verified as wheat-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is entirely pork-focused; pork fat, pork-based miso sauce, and deep-frying in shared oil used for pork cutlets make any vegan cross-contact unavoidable.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen-accommodation procedures, allergy menus, or staff allergy-handling protocols are published on the Tabelog listing, official website, or Kinshachi Yokocho shop page. A Yahoo Chiebukuro report notes that a customer with a dairy allergy was told by Yabaton that their fried items contain milk components, suggesting the kitchen does respond to direct inquiries but has no documented systematic allergy protocol.

positives.text: Cards and electronic payments accepted (VISA, MC, JCB, AMEX, Diners, Suica, iD, QUICPay, etc.) — easy for foreign visitors without cash.

cultural_comfort.note: Located in the tourist-facing Kinshachi Yokocho food district adjacent to Nagoya Castle; staff are accustomed to foreign visitors and the menu is visually accessible with photos.

places.name: 鮨 極 はなれ (Sushi Kiwami Hanare)
places.gf_detail: Standard Japanese soy sauce (contains wheat) is used for brushing nigiri, which is the norm for Edomae sushi. Shari (sushi rice) is seasoned with red vinegar — rice and vinegar are naturally gluten-free, but no tamari or wheat-free soy sauce alternative is advertised. Tempura does not appear on the menu. No dedicated GF policy or allergy menu is mentioned on any platform. Guests with celiac disease should contact the restaurant in advance to discuss whether tamari can be substituted; cross-contamination from shared prep surfaces cannot be ruled out without direct confirmation.
places.vegan_detail: Edomae nigiri is the entire menu concept — fish, seafood, tamagoyaki, and eel are the core items. No plant-based alternatives are listed anywhere. Hidden animal products (dashi, bonito, fish roe) are present throughout. Not a viable option for vegans.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Sun & holidays 12:00–15:00 / 17:30–23:00, closed Mon (also closed 2nd and 4th Sundays)
places.neighborhood: 国際センター駅 / 円頓寺横丁 (Nishi-ku, Nakano)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Sushi Kiwami Hanare opened on 15 June 2023 as the seated spin-off of 立ち食い寿司 極 (Tachigui Sushi Kiwami), a popular standing sushi bar near Nagoya Station. The parent shop earned a following for making skilled Edomae technique accessible and affordable in a standing format; this 'ha

chef_bio.origin: Entoji Yokocho, Nishi-ku, Nagoya (円頓寺横丁)
chef_bio.background: Sushi Kiwami Hanare opened on 15 June 2023 as the seated spin-off of 立ち食い寿司 極 (Tachigui Sushi Kiwami), a popular standing sushi bar near Nagoya Station. The parent shop earned a following for making skilled Edomae technique accessible and affordable in a standing format; this 'hanare' (annex) branch moves into a quiet alley of the Entoji Yokocho district in Nishi-ku, giving the same artisans a roughly ten-seat room (a six-seat counter plus a couple of small tables) where they can work at a more deliberate pace.
chef_bio.philosophy: A calm, adult, modern-Japanese room so every guest receives the chef's attention; seasonal fish selected daily; the red-vinegar (赤酢) shari is a signature across the Kiwami family and considered the foundation of the flavour profile.
chef_bio.specialty: Edomae nigiri using seasonal fish — with reviewers singling out fatty nodoguro (blackthroat seaperch) and kinmedai (alfonsino) — over red-vinegar shari.

anecdotes.text: A reviewer who visited in July 2023 described it as 'the seated Kiwami — a must for sake lovers,' noting the counter setting transforms the standing-bar spirit of the original into an intimate, adult dining experience.

An August 2024 visitor highlighted the nodoguro as 'incredibly fatty — it just melts,' and praised the kinmedai equally, with the red-vinegar shari called out as the defining characteristic of the Kiwami style.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 食べログ・金鯱系サイトによると、2023年6月15日、名駅近くの人気立ち食い寿司『極』が座って味わう『はなれ』として西区・円頓寺横丁に開業。約10席（カウンター6＋小テーブル）、赤酢のシャリを軸にした江戸前握り。のどぐろ・金目鯛等をレビューが称賛。

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information on cross-contamination controls is published. Shared prep surfaces and utensils in a small sushi counter are a realistic concern for celiac guests.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Edomae nigiri is traditionally brushed or served with standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat. No tamari or wheat-free soy sauce alternative is mentioned on any platform.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Seafood, fish, tamagoyaki, and eel are the entirety of the menu; no plant-based items or vegan accommodations exist.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy menu, gluten-free policy, or allergen labelling is documented on Tabelog, Gnavi, HotPepper, or the parent brand website. Guests with dietary restrictions should call ahead.

positives.text: The shari (sushi rice) is made with red vinegar and plain rice — both inherently gluten-free ingredients — so the rice base itself poses no gluten risk absent cross-contamination.

cultural_comfort.note: Intimate 10-seat counter with no English signage noted; the omakase-style format and no English menu make prior Japanese communication or a Japanese-speaking companion advisable.

places.name: カフェ ド SaRa (Cafe de SaRa)
places.gf_detail: Toast is the signature offering — wheat bread is central to the menu (black sesame toast, brown sugar toast, egg toast). No gluten-free menu, labeling, or awareness noted across multiple review sources. The kitchen is not celiac-safe. Customers with celiac disease or serious wheat allergy should not eat here.
places.vegan_detail: A traditional Nagoya kissaten focused on charcoal-roasted coffee and toast morning sets. The menu is built around wheat-based toast (black sesame, brown sugar, egg toast) and Western-style sweets such as chiffon cake, cheesecake, crème brûlée, and chocolate gateau. No vegan labeling or plant-based orientation found. Dairy and eggs appear throughout the menu. Dashi and other hidden animal products in sweets cannot be ruled out. Vegan diners should ask before ordering.
places.hours_raw: Mon & Thu 8:45–13:00 (may close at 12:00 when items sell out); Tue/Wed/Fri/Sat 8:45–16:00; Sun & holidays closed; irregular additional closures announced via Instagram
places.neighborhood: Nishi-ku, Nagono/Shikemichi historic merchant district
places.cuisine: Specialty coffee, morning service toast, Nag
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Cafe de SaRa occupies a renovated townhouse estimated at 120 years old or more in Shikemichi (四間道), Nagoya's best-preserved Edo-period merchant street in Nishi-ku. The building's exposed beams and alley setting earned recognition as part of the city's landscape heritage. The owne

chef_bio.background: Cafe de SaRa occupies a renovated machiya townhouse (listed as over a century old; some sites say 120 years or more) in Shikemichi (四間道), Nagoya's best-preserved Edo-period merchant street in Nishi-ku. The building's exposed timber earned recognition through a Nagoya city landscape award (都市景観賞). The owner runs the cafe single-handedly.
chef_bio.philosophy: Slow, attentive coffee service in a space that values quiet and unhurried time — the kissaten tradition at its most considered.
chef_bio.specialty: Charcoal-roasted coffee (炭火焙煎) hand-ground and brewed one cup at a time by paper drip, paired with a morning toast set — the half-and-half black sesame and brown sugar toast (with crushed egg and homemade jam) is the signature. Coffee is served in rotating high-end dishware including Hermès and Herend cups. Iced coffee arrives with a companion glass of extra coffee so the melting ice will not dilute the cup.

anecdotes.text: The 100-plus-year-old machiya, with its exposed timber interior preserved during renovation, received a Nagoya city landscape award (都市景観賞). The owner works alone, so patience is part of the experience.
In the Nagoya kissaten tradition, the weekday morning service adds a toast set to the price of a drink at no extra charge, and coffee is typically accompanied by a small complimentary sweet.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Retty lists the building as 築120年以上; jouhou.nagoya and nagoya.xtone confirm the Shikemichi machiya (100年以上), charcoal-roasted coffee, solo owner, and the city landscape award (市景観賞); juno.nagoya confirms the black-sesame/brown-sugar toast morning set and Hermès/Herend cups.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Toast — wheat bread — is the core product of this cafe. Flour is present throughout the kitchen. No allergen controls or gluten-free procedures have been noted anywhere in available sources. This venue is not appropriate for anyone with celiac disease.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dairy and eggs appear throughout the menu (butter on toast, egg toast, cream-based sweets). No vegan accommodation has been documented.

positives.text: Entirely non-smoking interior.

cultural_comfort.note: A solo-operated Japanese kissaten with no English signage or menu noted; basic pointing or simple Japanese phrases will be needed, though the quiet, unhurried atmosphere is welcoming to visitors.

places.name: レイヤーズ 丸の内 本店 (LAYER'S Marunouchi Honten)
places.gf_detail: Standard wheat-flour burger buns are the core of every item on the menu and there is no gluten-free bun alternative documented anywhere. Fries and other sides are prepared in a shared kitchen environment; no dedicated fryer information is available. Soy sauce use in sauces cannot be ruled out. Not safe for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: All burgers are built around 100% beef patties (Australian and Hida beef blend) with dairy cheese as standard; no plant-based patty or dairy-free option has been documented on any source reviewed. Buns are house-made and contain wheat and likely dairy/egg. Effectively no vegan path.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–22:00 (L.O. 21:00), irregular days off
places.neighborhood: Marunouchi / ~5–6 min walk from Hisaya-ōdōri Stn
places.notes: LAYER'S opened in Nagoya's Marunouchi district around 2006 and has grown into one of the city's most celebrated gourmet burger destinations, earning a place on Tabelog's Hamburger Top 100 list repeatedly, including for 2026. The founder trained at the respected Tokyo burger shop

chef_bio.background: The founder trained at the respected Tokyo burger shop BROTHERZ before establishing this location.
chef_bio.philosophy: Emphasis on materials and handcraft: the 'LAYER'S original' buns are hand-made one by one each day and faintly sweet, and the beef blend is chosen for both flavour and quality. The kitchen has also produced a number of independent burger chefs — reported as around eight in Nagoya — who have gone on to open their own shops.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature LAYER'S Burger — a towering stack built on house-made, lightly sweetened buns crafted fresh daily, filled with a 100% beef patty blended from Australian beef and Hida wagyu, plus lettuce, onion, cheese, bacon, pineapple, and egg, finished with a choice of BBQ, teriyaki, red hot chili, or sweet chili sauce.

anecdotes.text: The owner trained at BROTHERZ, a respected Tokyo burger establishment, before bringing the concept to Nagoya — a pedigree that underpins the shop's reputation for technical craft.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: nagoya-meshi and michidesign confirm the BROTHERZ pedigree, hand-made daily 'LAYER'S original' buns (ほんのりした甘さ, 毎日一枚一枚手作り), Australian+Hida-wagyu 100% patty, the shop having produced ~8 独立者 in Nagoya, and repeated Tabelog 百名店 selection.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated gluten-free fryer or preparation area is documented. Wheat buns are central to every menu item and the kitchen is a standard burger operation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sauce ingredients (BBQ, teriyaki, chili variants) are not publicly disclosed; teriyaki sauce in Japanese kitchens typically contains wheat-based soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All patties are beef; cheese is standard on most burgers. No plant-based items exist to assess cross-contact.

cultural_comfort.note: Walk-in casual burger shop with no English menu; staff are accustomed to local clientele, but pointing at a menu or using a translation app will get you served without difficulty.

places.name: 美味しいごはん じらふ (Oishii Gohan Jirafu)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free awareness or labeling found anywhere on the official site, Tabelog, or review sources. Core menu items are high-gluten: kushiage uses four types of breadcrumbs (wheat-based), karaage is battered, and the house yakibuta sauce is a soy-based tare (standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat). No dedicated fryer information available; the same fryer almost certainly handles breadcrumbed kushiage. Celiac diners should not eat here without detailed staff consultation in Japanese — and even then, cross-contamination risk from frying is very high.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or labeling exists. Signature dishes center on Aichi Mikawa pork, local fried chicken, and kushiage (battered, deep-fried skewers). The special yakibuta sauce is soy-based and has been maintained since opening — likely contains wheat. Dashi type is unconfirmed but izakaya-style cooking typically uses katsuobushi-based dashi. Off-menu requests are accepted depending on available ingredients, so a diner willing to communicate needs in Japanese may find limited options, but nothing can be guaranteed without direct inquiry.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Fri 11:30–14:00 (lunch), 17:00–22:00 (dinner); Sat 17:00–22:00; closed Sun & holidays
places.neighborhood: Marunouchi / Hisaya-ōdōri
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The owner-chef has over 20 years of culinary experience and runs this intimate izakaya in Nagoya's Marunouchi business district, conveniently positioned between Hisaya-odori and Marunouchi subway stations.

chef_bio.background: The owner-chef runs this intimate 42-seat izakaya in Nagoya's Marunouchi business district, conveniently positioned between Hisaya-odori (4 min) and Marunouchi (5 min) subway stations.
chef_bio.philosophy: Handmade creative cuisine that pairs well with alcohol; the chef will prepare off-menu dishes when ingredients allow, making each visit feel personal.
chef_bio.specialty: 愛知みかわ豚の特製焼豚 — Aichi Mikawa pork that is steamed, pressure-cooked, then grilled and served with a house soy-tare sauce continuously replenished (継ぎ足し) since the restaurant opened. Also known for local Oku-Mikawa chicken karaage and a rotating kushiage assortment using seasonal ingredients and multiple breadcrumb types.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's operating motto, noted on the official site, is that service continues 'as long as the chef's stamina permits' — a candid nod to the one-person-driven nature of the kitchen.
The special yakibuta tare (sauce) has been kept alive and continuously added to since the restaurant first opened, a technique reminiscent of long-running ramen or unagi tare.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Official site (giraffe-marunouchi.com) confirms the 'as long as the chef's stamina permits' motto, 42 seats, Marunouchi/Hisaya-odori access, and the Mikawa-pork yakibuta (steamed→pressure-cooked→grilled) with tare 継ぎ足し since opening, plus Oku-Mikawa karaage and seasonal kushiage. Hitosara names the owner-chef 安藤時久.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Kushiage (deep-fried skewers) is a core menu category using four types of breadcrumbs, almost certainly wheat-based. The fryer is very likely shared with other fried items including karaage. No dedicated fryer information found.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature yakibuta dish uses a soy-based tare that has been maintained since opening. Standard Japanese soy sauce (koikuchi shoyu) contains wheat. No tamari or gluten-free soy sauce alternative is mentioned anywhere.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Pork, chicken, and egg dishes dominate the menu. The frying oil is shared with animal-based kushiage. No vegan-safe cooking zones or oils confirmed.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The owner-chef accepts off-menu requests when ingredients are available, suggesting some flexibility, but no allergy protocol or dietary accommodation system is described on any public source. Communication in Japanese is essential.

positives.text: Owner-chef with 20+ years of experience runs a small, personal kitchen — direct communication about dietary needs is more feasible than at a chain restaurant.

cultural_comfort.note: A small neighborhood izakaya catering to Marunouchi office workers; menus and signage are in Japanese only, and while the owner is accommodating, communicating dietary restrictions will require Japanese or translation assistance.

places.name: いねのや (Inenoya)
places.gf_detail: Soba is listed alongside udon and ramen, so shared cooking water and wheat cross-contamination are near-certain. The restaurant's tsuyu is conventional and wheat-containing soy sauce is standard practice here. No allergen menu, no GF labeling, no staff allergy protocols are mentioned on any source. Not safe for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan options identified. The menu centers on soba and udon served with standard fish-based dashi (katsuobushi/niboshi), plus ramen and donburi. No plant-based broth alternatives are mentioned on any source.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Fri, Sat, Sun 11:00–14:00 / 17:00–20:00, closed Wed and Thu
places.neighborhood: 浄心 / 名西 (Nishi Ward)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Soba is listed alongside udon and ramen, so shared cooking water and wheat cross-contamination are near-certain. The restaurant's tsuyu is conventional and wheat-containing soy sauce is standard practice here. No allergen menu, no GF labeling, no staff allergy protocols are menti

chef_bio.background: A neighborhood soba/udon shop in Nagoya's Nishi Ward, near Jōjin Station, serving handmade noodles alongside rice-bowl sets. Miso-nikomi udon, a Nagoya specialty, is also on the menu.
chef_bio.specialty: Ebi-katsu don (shrimp-cutlet rice bowl) and chilled tanuki soba are the dishes most praised in visitor posts; the lunch sets are noted for generous portions at modest prices.

anecdotes.text: A visitor described a chilled tanuki soba set (¥1,200) featuring springy, chilled buckwheat noodles with crisp tenkasu, a rich tsuyu, and a side potato croquette — calling it reliable neighborhood value.
The weekday mini-curry-don lunch set (¥1,000) drew praise for its stable, well-seasoned Japanese-style curry.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog places Inenoya in Nagoya Nishi-ku near Jōjin Station and confirms ebi-katsu don, chilled tanuki soba (with tenkasu), curry-don lunch, and miso-nikomi udon at modest (sub-¥1,000 to ~¥1,500) prices.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Soba is cooked alongside udon and ramen in a small kitchen; shared boiling water and utensils make wheat cross-contamination very likely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No tamari or GF soy sauce is mentioned; standard wheat-containing soy sauce is used in the tsuyu and dashi, as is universal practice at this type of conventional soba-ya.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is fish-based (katsuo/niboshi dashi standard for this style); no plant-based broth alternative is offered.

cultural_comfort.note: A quiet, old-fashioned neighborhood soba shop with a local clientele and no English signage or menu; basic Japanese helpful for ordering.

places.name: 呑ひゃら (Donhyara)
places.gf_detail: Tempura is the core offering — batter is wheat flour. Standard Japanese soy sauce (containing wheat) is almost certainly used in sauces and marinades. A shared fryer handles shrimp, fish, and vegetables together, so cross-contamination is inherent to the operation. No gluten-free options, dedicated fryer, or allergen accommodation has been reported anywhere. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: This is a tempura-focused izakaya. Tempura batter is wheat-based and the frying oil is shared across shrimp, fish, and vegetable items. Miso soup accompanies most sets and likely contains katsuobushi (bonito) dashi. No vegan menu exists; vegetable-only ordering may be possible at dinner with advance notice but cannot be confirmed. Ask directly when reserving.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Fri 11:30–12:45 (L.O. around 12:00) / 17:30–21:00 (L.O. 20:00), closed Sat/Sun/holidays
places.neighborhood: Nishi-ku, Sengen-chō, 2 min walk from Sengen-chō Stn
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A long-established neighborhood izakaya in Nagoya's Nishi Ward, a short walk from Senamamachi Station. The restaurant is run by an elderly couple and occupies a modest, traditional building with a distinctly Showa-era atmosphere.

chef_bio.background: A long-established neighborhood izakaya in Nagoya's Nishi Ward, roughly a 10-minute walk from Sengen-chō (浅間町) Station on the Tsurumai Line. The restaurant is run by an elderly couple and occupies a modest, traditional building with a distinctly Showa-era atmosphere.
chef_bio.philosophy: Simple, honest tempura crafted to order by a skilled master; lunch is limited to about four items and runs only around 75 minutes, with dinner seats booking out months in advance.
chef_bio.specialty: Tempura rice bowls — especially the oversized kakiage-don (mixed vegetable fritter bowl) whose fritter famously extends well beyond the rim of the bowl — and the upper-grade ebi tendon.

anecdotes.text: The kakiage fritter is described by visitors as comically large — it domes high over the bowl — yet the oil feels light and the batter stays crispy (サクサクふわふわ) throughout the meal.
Dinner reservations reportedly require a wait of around three months, a remarkable waitlist for a roughly 16-seat neighborhood izakaya.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: oinagoya confirms Nishi-ku near Sengen-chō (浅間町) Station, elderly-couple operation, Showa atmosphere, ~16 seats, ~4-item ~75-minute lunch, dinner booked months ahead, and the oversized crispy kakiage-don.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tempura is the restaurant's core offering; shrimp, fish, and vegetables are fried together in shared oil. Cross-contamination with shellfish and wheat is inherent and unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce (which contains wheat) is the expected base for tempura dipping sauce (tentsuyu) and any seasoning used at this izakaya. No tamari or GF soy sauce alternatives have been mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Shrimp and fish are fried in the same oil as vegetable tempura; miso soup served with lunch sets likely uses katsuobushi (bonito) dashi. No vegan-separated preparation has been reported.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No information about allergy handling, allergen menus, or staff training for dietary restrictions was found across any source reviewed.

positives.text: Dinner is reservation-only and the small team takes each order seriously; advance reservation provides an opportunity to communicate dietary needs directly to the cook before arrival.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny Showa-era counter-and-table izakaya run by an elderly couple; no English menu or signage, weekday-only hours with dinner by reservation months in advance — comfortable for Japanese-speaking diners or confident regulars.

places.name: 桔梗 (Kikyo)
places.gf_detail: The soba is hand-cut fresh each morning from Hokkaido buckwheat flour, but wheat is used in the soba dipping sauce (soy sauce), tempura batter (wheat), and various dishes. If ordering pure soba only, it may be 100% buckwheat flour, but there is no statement whatsoever regarding celiac accommodation — whether it is jūwari (100%) soba, whether the dipping sauce can be avoided, or the cross-contamination risk from cookware used with wheat flour. Gluten-sensitive diners should be sure to confirm before visiting.
places.vegan_detail: A typical soba izakaya, where the dashi is standardly bonito/kelp-based. The menu makes heavy use of animal ingredients in tempura, simmered dishes, and sake snacks. No explicit vegan accommodation. The hand-cut Hokkaido-buckwheat soba itself is plant-based, but the dipping sauce (kaeshi plus bonito dashi) is animal-based. There are seasonal vegetable dishes, but avoiding hidden animal ingredients requires inquiry.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Fri 11:00–14:00 / 17:00–23:00 (L.O. 22:00); closed Sat, Sun, and holidays
places.neighborhood: Marunouchi, Naka Ward (1 min walk from Marunouchi Station)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A hand-cut soba and Japanese cuisine restaurant a 1-minute walk from a Marunouchi, Nagoya subway exit, tucked into a corner of the business district. The main location has 70 seats on the 1st floor of the C.I. Mansion Marunouchi, and there is also an annex, "Hanare," a reservation-only building near the main location repurposed from a 160-year-old historic structure.

chef_bio.background: 名古屋・丸の内の地下鉄出口から徒歩1分、ビジネス街の一角に構える手打ち蕎麦と日本料理の店。本店はシーアイマンション丸の内1Fに70席を擁し、さらに本店近くに築160年の歴史的建造物を転用した完全予約制の別棟「はなれ」を持つ。
chef_bio.philosophy: 毎日柳橋市場で仕入れる旬の鮮魚・野菜と手打ち蕎麦を軸に、日本酒・焼酎とともに楽しむ「蕎麦居酒屋」スタイルを追求。
chef_bio.specialty: 北海道産蕎麦粉を毎朝製粉・手打ちした蕎麦。コシと香りが特徴で、薬味には信州産「親田辛味大根」を使用。

anecdotes.text: 別棟「はなれ」は築160年の情緒ある建物を活かした完全予約制の個室空間。ビジネス接待から少人数の会食まで対応し、都心の喧騒を忘れる静謐な雰囲気を提供する。

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 公式サイトとホットペッパーが、丸の内（中区）徒歩1分・シーアイマンション1Fの70席本店、柳橋市場からの毎日仕入れ、北海道産そば粉の毎朝手打ち、信州産親田辛味大根、および築160年の完全予約制別棟「はなれ」を確認。

gf_cross_contamination.text: 天ぷらなど小麦粉を使用するメニューを提供しており、揚げ油・調理器具の共用による交差汚染リスクについての情報は一切公開されていない。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: そばつゆに使用される醤油は通常小麦を含む。グルテンフリー醤油（丸大豆醤油等）使用の記載なし。

vegan_cross_contact.text: かつおや昆布を使った出汁が料理全般に使われている蕎麦居酒屋。ヴィーガン向けの出汁分離・調理分離についての情報なし。

staff_allergy_handling.text: アレルギー・食事制限への対応方針について、公式サイト・各グルメサイトのいずれにも具体的な記載なし。来店前に直接電話での確認を推奨（052-201-1559、電話受付は15〜17時を避けること）。

positives.text: 毎朝手打ちの蕎麦は北海道産蕎麦粉100%使用の可能性があり、純粋な蕎麦のみ注文・つゆ別添えで対応できるかは要確認。

cultural_comfort.note: 週末は定休で平日ビジネス客が中心。英語メニュー・英語対応の記載なし。基本的に日本語でのコミュニケーションが必要。

places.name: メゼス サントリーニ (Mezes Santorini)
places.gf_detail: Greek cuisine avoids soy sauce and dashi, which removes two common Japanese gluten vectors. However, moussaka uses a wheat-flour béchamel, Greek pies (spanakopita-style) use phyllo, and no gluten-free menu or dedicated fryer is mentioned anywhere. The kitchen is small and likely shared. No source confirms GF awareness or accommodation. Diners with celiac should call in advance to ask which dishes are flour-free.
places.vegan_detail: Greek cuisine here centers on charcoal-grilled meats, lamb, seafood, and dairy-rich preparations like moussaka (béchamel) and yogurt sauces. Olive oil, fresh vegetables, and marinated seafood feature prominently, and some cold mezze plates may be naturally plant-based, but no menu is explicitly labeled vegan. Dairy (feta, yogurt, béchamel) appears throughout. Best to call ahead and ask.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sat 11:00–14:00 / 17:00–22:00 (L.O. 21:00); closed Sun. Course reservations up to 3 days in advance.
places.neighborhood: Around Sengen-chō Station (Jōsai, Nishi Ward, Nagoya)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened in October 2016 as Nagoya's only Greek restaurant. A small standalone house run by a Greek owner-chef and his Japanese wife. Using an old row-house building a few minutes' walk from Sengen-chō Station, it creates a calm space with Middle Eastern music playing.

roles: オーナーシェフ（ギリシャ人男性）
chef_bio.origin: 名古屋市西区城西、浅間町駅周辺
chef_bio.background: 名古屋唯一のギリシャ料理店として2016年10月にオープン。ギリシャ人のオーナーシェフと日本人の奥様によって営まれる小さな一軒家レストラン。浅間町駅から徒歩数分の古い建築を使い、落ち着いた空間を作り出している。
chef_bio.philosophy: 地中海食の原則に基づき、オリーブオイル・新鮮な野菜・魚介類・肉類をバランスよく使う「メゼス（小皿料理）」のスタイルでギリシャ本国の味を再現することを目指している。
chef_bio.specialty: 炭火焼ミックスグリル（仔羊・ソーセージなど）およびムサカ（ナス・ポテト・ミートソース・ベシャメルソースの重ね焼き）。ギリシャ産エクストラバージンオリーブオイルを使用。

anecdotes.text: ギリシャ人オーナーはギリシャ本国の料理法を持ち込み、「日本にいながら本物のギリシャ料理を」というコンセプトで開業。レビュアーからは本国さながらの味と評されている。
ムサカはキッシュのように切り分けて提供され、ヨーグルトソースとの組み合わせで「コクが増す」と評判。肉料理は炭火焼きにこだわる。

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog confirms Mezes Santorini as Nagoya's only Greek restaurant (名古屋で唯一), opened 2016年10月11日, at 名古屋市西区城西 near 浅間町駅, in an older building, run by a Greek owner-chef, specializing in charcoal grill, moussaka, and Greek olive oil.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information on kitchen cross-contamination practices was found in any source. The kitchen appears small (30-seat restaurant in a converted townhouse), raising the likelihood of shared surfaces.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Greek cuisine does not use soy sauce or dashi as base ingredients, which eliminates the most common Japanese gluten/hidden-fish vectors. However, wheat flour is used in béchamel (moussaka) and likely in Greek pies.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Charcoal grill is used for both meat and seafood; no separation from plant-based items confirmed. Dairy (feta, yogurt, béchamel) is central to the menu.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No source mentions any allergy-handling policy, allergen menu, or staff training for dietary restrictions. English communication is noted as possible with the Greek owner.

positives.text: Greek cuisine base avoids soy sauce, mirin, dashi, and other umami seasonings standard in Japanese kitchens, making it structurally safer from several common hidden-gluten and hidden-fish sources.
Olive-oil-based cooking and fresh vegetable/seafood focus means some dishes (marinated octopus, grilled fish, salads) may be naturally GF — but this must be verified per dish.

cultural_comfort.note: Staff includes both a Greek owner and a Japanese spouse; some English is available, but the menu and setting are primarily Japanese-language.

places.name: 越廼屋 (Koshinoya)
places.gf_detail: The cutlet in the sauce-katsudon uses wheat breadcrumbs. The Echizen oroshi soba may be purely buckwheat flour (some reviews mention "jūwari" / 100%), but there is no official information at all about wheat contamination in the dashi or soy sauce, or cross-contamination risk in the kitchen. Gluten-free accommodation is not stated, so it is unsuitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: The Echizen oroshi soba is topped with bonito flakes. The sauce-katsudon is mainly pork cutlet (lard, pork). Details of the dashi and soy sauce are unknown, but animal ingredients are used throughout, and there is no vegan menu.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Fri 11:30–14:00 (L.O. 13:45); Sat irregular closures (confirm on Facebook); closed Sun and holidays
places.neighborhood: Marunouchi, Naka Ward (1–4 min walk from Marunouchi Station)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened April 13, 2015. The owner is from Fukui Prefecture and opened this shop to bring Fukui's regional cuisine to Nagoya's business district. A small lunch-only spot on the first floor of a building right by Marunouchi subway station.

chef_bio.background: 2015年4月13日開業。オーナーは福井県にゆかりがあり（屋号「越廼屋」は福井の越廼＝こしのに由来）、福井の郷土料理を名古屋のビジネス街に届けるべくこの店を開いた。地下鉄丸の内駅4番出口すぐ（徒歩15秒）のビル1階に構える小さなランチ専門店。
chef_bio.philosophy: 地元福井の味を正直に再現する姿勢。ランチのみの営業で、食材・仕込みの質を保つ。
chef_bio.specialty: 福井ソースカツ丼と越前おろしそば。薄く大きなロース肉にきめ細かいパン粉をまとわせ、濃厚なウスターソースにくぐらせたカツを丼飯の上に載せた一品と、鰹節・青ねぎ・大根おろしをたっぷり乗せた清涼感のある越前おろしそばをセットで味わうのが定番。

anecdotes.text: 福井出身者が試食して「本物の味」と太鼓判を押したと地元ブロガーが報告している。

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog confirms 2015年4月13日開業, 中区丸の内（地下鉄4番出口徒歩15秒）, ランチ専門, and the Fukui specialties ソースカツ丼・越前おろしそば. The 越廼屋 name references Fukui's Koshino, consistent with the owner's Fukui roots reported by local blogs.

gf_cross_contamination.text: ソースカツ丼の揚げ物と蕎麦を同一厨房で調理しており、グルテン交差汚染のリスクが高い。専用フライヤーの有無は未確認。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: 出汁・つゆへの醤油使用が想定されるが、小麦含有の有無について公式情報なし。

vegan_cross_contact.text: かつお節を越前おろしそばのトッピングに使用しており、動物性食材を全メニューで使用。ヴィーガン対応不可。

cultural_comfort.note: 小さなランチ専門店でメニューは日本語のみ、英語対応の記録なし。

places.name: 天婦羅 稲葉 (Tenpura Inaba)
places.gf_detail: Standard tempura batter is wheat flour — this is not a GF-friendly format by default. Tentsuyu dipping broth contains wheat-based soy sauce. The restaurant requests allergy information be communicated at the time of reservation as same-day changes are difficult; whether wheat-free alternatives can be arranged is unknown and must be confirmed directly before booking.
places.vegan_detail: Tempura is traditionally fried in sesame/vegetable oil (potentially vegan-compatible frying medium), but tentsuyu dipping sauce is dashi-based (likely bonito/katsuobushi). No plant-based or vegan course is advertised. Allergy and dietary restrictions must be communicated at reservation time; viability for strict vegans is unconfirmed.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30–15:00 (L.O. 13:00), dinner two seatings at 17:30 and 20:30 (fully reservation-only), irregular days off
places.neighborhood: Nishi-ku, Hinokuchi-chō (~7 min walk from Sengen-chō Stn)
places.notes: Chef Masanobu Inaba was born in Tokyo in 1967 and entered professional Japanese cuisine at age 18 after his family's sushi restaurant faced financial difficulties. He is largely self-taught, rising without the conventional pathway through prestigious establishments. He served as

chef_bio.origin: Tokyo (Ginza flagship); Nagoya Owari location (尾張 粋 稲葉) opened October 1, 2025 inside the newly built Espacio Nagoya Castle luxury hotel, the group's first venue outside Tokyo.
chef_bio.background: Chef Masanobu Inaba was born in Tokyo in 1967 and entered professional cuisine at age 18 after his family's sushi restaurant hit financial difficulties. Rather than the traditional ryotei apprenticeship, he rose through top hotel kitchens: vice head chef at Grand Hyatt Tokyo's sushi counter, executive chef at Conrad Tokyo's Kazahana Japanese restaurant, and head chef at the Amanemu (Aman) luxury resort in Ise-Shima.
chef_bio.philosophy: 温故知新 — treasuring heritage while treating tradition as a continuous series of innovations. Inaba pays close attention to seasonal ingredients, appropriate dishware, timing, temperature, and each guest's comfort to create a special, blissful experience.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal omakase tempura: each piece fried individually with technique matched to the ingredient, served at a 6-seat hinoki counter (Espacio Nagoya Castle 4F) where guests watch the chef work. Dinner is an omakase course from ¥33,000.

anecdotes.text: The breakfast Inaba developed at Amanemu in Ise-Shima was recognized by US magazine Brides as one of 'The Best Hotel Breakfasts in the world' — an unusual international accolade for a Japanese inn-style morning meal.
Inaba established Ginza Inaba in July 2021 in partnership with MUGEN's Naohiro Uchiyama during the COVID-19 pandemic.
The Nagoya venue, 尾張 粋 稲葉, marks the group's first venue outside Tokyo, housed inside Espacio Nagoya Castle — a luxury hotel that opened October 1, 2025 on the site of the former Hotel Nagoya Castle.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: MUGEN's takumi profile confirms born Tokyo 1967, entry at 18 after family sushi troubles, Grand Hyatt Tokyo/Conrad Tokyo/Amanemu roles, the Brides breakfast accolade, and Ginza Inaba established July 2021 with Uchiyama Naohiro. jouhou.nagoya and the official Owari page confirm 尾張 粋 稲葉 (tempura/kaiseki, 6-seat hinoki counter, 4F) at Espacio Nagoya Castle, opened Oct 1 2025 on the former Hotel Nagoya Castle site.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tempura batter is made from wheat flour by definition; a dedicated GF fryer or wheat-free batter is not mentioned anywhere on the restaurant's site or in any review source found.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tentsuyu dipping sauce is standard dashi + mirin + soy sauce (wheat-containing); no tamari or wheat-free soy sauce alternative is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dipping broth (tentsuyu) is almost certainly bonito-dashi based, the standard for tempura restaurants. No plant-based dashi alternative is mentioned.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The official site states: 'Allergies and other dietary restrictions should be communicated in advance, as same-day substitutions are difficult to accommodate.' This implies some flexibility if flagged at booking, but no specifics are given.

positives.text: Completely reservation-only format with advance allergy disclosure requirement means the kitchen has time to prepare; a 6-seat counter gives chefs full control over each guest's experience and direct knowledge of individual needs.

cultural_comfort.note: All materials and reservations are in Japanese; staff may have limited English, and the formal omakase setting assumes familiarity with traditional tempura counter etiquette.

places.name: 馬力屋 (Barikiya)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free claims anywhere. Standard Japanese soy sauce (contains wheat) is almost certainly used in the tare and marinades. The stamina teppan uses hacho miso, which may also contain wheat. No allergen menu, no dedicated fryer information, and no staff allergy-handling protocol documented. Celiac diners should call ahead and approach with caution.
places.vegan_detail: This is a meat-only yakiniku and horumon specialist. Every dish centres on beef offal, pork, or other animal proteins. There are no plant-based mains, and the kitchen has no documented vegan provisions.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Wed–Sun 17:00–23:00 (L.O. 22:00), closed Tue and the 3rd Wednesday
places.neighborhood: Nishi-ku, Shindō (7 min walk from Sengen-chō Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Barikiya has been serving the working-class Shindo neighbourhood of Nagoya's Nishi Ward since around 1970, making it one of the older surviving horumon-yaki joints in the area. The shop's blackened exterior, red lanterns, and perpetual haze of charcoal smoke mark it as a relic of

chef_bio.background: Barikiya has been serving the Shindo (新道) neighbourhood of Nagoya's Nishi Ward, near Sengen-chō Station on the Tsurumai Line, since around 1970 — one of the older surviving horumon-yaki joints in the area, with a perpetual haze of charcoal smoke and a Showa-era downtown feel.
chef_bio.philosophy: Honest, unglamorous yakiniku at neighbourhood prices: fresh offal served in a no-frills room where the meat does the talking.
chef_bio.specialty: Ninniku Sagari — thick-cut beef hanging tender blanketed in raw grated garlic, grilled table-side until the garlic mellows and caramelises on the hot grate.

anecdotes.text: Regulars describe the interior's twin high-power extractor fans running to cope with the smoke from flaring horumon — small intestine famously flames up on the griddle, filling the compact room with a satisfying char.

The finishing move is the garlic fried rice, built on the same punchy garlic base as the sagari and described by one blogger as wholly different from any Chinese-style fried rice — a distinctly Barikiya creation.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: deladesign confirms Barikiya in Nishi-ku's Shindo neighborhood near Sengen-chō Station, Showa atmosphere since roughly 1970, signature ninniku sagari (garlic-topped hanging tender) and garlic fried rice, flamboyant offal on the grill, and dual high-power fans for the smoke.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No fryer information is documented. The kitchen handles marinated meats and offal; cross-contamination risk from shared equipment is unknown.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No allergen menu exists. Standard Japanese soy sauce containing wheat is the expected baseline for yakiniku tare and marinades at this type of establishment — no wheat-free soy sauce or tamari is mentioned anywhere.

vegan_cross_contact.text: This is an all-meat establishment; every surface and grill is in contact with animal products. No plant-based preparation area exists.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy-handling protocol has been documented for this restaurant. Phone reservations are accepted and calling ahead (052-551-0598) in Japanese is the only known channel for allergy enquiries.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, smoky neighbourhood spot with a decades-long local following; no English signage or menu, phone reservations in Japanese only.

places.name: ププキッチン (Pupu Kitchen)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu or certification. The menu is rice-based (enzyme brown rice) and naturally avoids many wheat sources, but standard soy sauce containing wheat is likely used in seasoning. No dedicated gluten-free fryer or kitchen separation confirmed. Celiac diners should ask specifically about soy sauce and any wheat-containing condiments before ordering. The macrobiotic framework does not inherently exclude wheat.
places.vegan_detail: The daily lunch set and curry lunch are fully plant-based (no meat, fish, eggs, or dairy). The desserts — including a soy-milk soft-serve parfait — are also egg-, dairy-, and refined-sugar-free. Standard soy sauce (containing wheat) is likely used in cooking; no tamari-specific claim confirmed. Dishes use macrobiotically sourced organic vegetables, whole grains (enzyme brown rice), and plant proteins such as takakibi (sorghum) and soy-based substitutes. Hidden animal products such as bonito dashi are not part of the macrobiotic philosophy, but explicit confirmation that all broth is vegan has not been found.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Sat 10:00–15:30 (L.O. 15:00), lunch 11:30–14:00, closed Sun and Mon
places.neighborhood: 城西 / Josai, Nishi Ward
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Healing Ai was founded in 1991 by health consultant Katoh Hiroko, who turned to macrobiotics and natural therapy after recovering from her own illness caused by nutritional excess. She passed the business to current owner Itoh Kazushige, who has continued her mission for over two

chef_bio.background: Healing Ai was founded in 1991 by health consultant Katoh Hiroko, who turned to macrobiotics and natural therapy after recovering from her own illness caused by nutritional excess. She passed the business to current owner Itoh Kazushige, who has continued her mission for over two decades — writing a monthly customer newsletter that grew to some 500 manuscript pages, later compiled into a published book titled '20年後のわたし' (Twenty Years Later). The establishment received the 3rd Japan Vegetarian Award in 2018 for its contribution to plant-based lifestyle promotion.
chef_bio.philosophy: Three macrobiotic principles guide every dish: 身土不二 (body and soil as one — eat local and seasonal), 一物全体 (use the whole ingredient), and 陰陽調和 (yin-yang balance). The café aims to correct imbalanced nutrition through Japanese traditional food, and stocks only products the owners personally believe in.
chef_bio.specialty: 毎日替わりプープーランチ (daily macrobiotic lunch set) — a single-plate meal featuring enzyme brown rice, miso soup, and rotating sides such as a takakibi (sorghum) hamburger, tofu terrine, hijiki marinade, and root-vegetable potage.

anecdotes.text: Founder Katoh Hiroko developed her health philosophy after personal illness revealed the dangers of nutritional excess; her recovery through macrobiotics became the founding story of Healing Ai in 1991.
The establishment won the 3rd Japan Vegetarian Award in 2018, recognizing its long-running commitment to promoting a plant-based lifestyle.
Owner Itoh has written a monthly 'Healing Ai Letter' for over 20 years — some 500 manuscript pages in total — which was compiled and published as a book, '20年後のわたし'.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Vegewel confirms Healing Ai founded 1991 by health consultant 加藤裕子 (recovered from illness of 栄養過多), current owner 伊藤和茂, a monthly newsletter totalling 500枚 compiled into the book『20年後のわたし』, and the 第3回日本ベジタリアンアワード (2018); location 名古屋市西区浅間町.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated gluten-free kitchen or fryer confirmed. The menu is naturally low in wheat due to its macrobiotic, rice-based focus, but cross-contamination from shared surfaces or utensils cannot be ruled out.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard soy sauce (which contains wheat) is likely used in seasoning, as no tamari or gluten-free soy sauce alternative is mentioned anywhere in available sources. Celiac diners must ask specifically about soy sauce used in cooking.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The café philosophy is fully plant-based — no meat, fish, eggs, or dairy are used in the main kitchen — so vegan cross-contact is very unlikely in normal operation.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Staff are trained macrobiotic practitioners knowledgeable about ingredients sourced from the adjacent natural foods store; however, no formal allergy protocol or allergen-menu documentation has been confirmed.

positives.text: All standard desserts are made without eggs, dairy, or refined sugar; soy-milk soft-serve is used throughout the sweets menu.
The daily lunch uses organic, pesticide-free vegetables and whole grains (enzyme brown rice); no animal products appear in the main menu.

cultural_comfort.note: An English-language menu is available, but the setting is a small local macrobiotic cafe embedded in a Japanese natural foods store — some familiarity with Japanese dining customs and basic phrases will make the visit smoother.

places.name: 龍園 (Ryuen)
places.gf_detail: Standard Japanese shoyu (soy sauce) is brewed with wheat and is the defining flavour of this bowl. No gluten-free soy sauce alternative is mentioned anywhere. The kitchen is a tiny counter operation with no dedicated allergen protocols on record. This shop is not safe for anyone with celiac disease or serious wheat allergy.
places.vegan_detail: The signature shoyu ramen is built on an animal-based broth (chicken and/or pork bone stock), and the soy sauce tare almost certainly contains wheat. No plant-based or vegan options are listed or described in any source. Vegans have no workable choice here.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Wed, Fri, Sat 11:30–13:30, closed Thu and Sun
places.neighborhood: Jōshin (Nishi-ku)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Standard Japanese shoyu (soy sauce) is brewed with wheat and is the defining flavour of this bowl. No gluten-free soy sauce alternative is mentioned anywhere. The kitchen is a tiny counter operation with no dedicated allergen protocols on record. This shop is not safe for anyone

chef_bio.background: A small 10-seat counter-only shop in a residential part of Nagoya's Nishi Ward.
chef_bio.philosophy: Reviewers describe the broth as 'gentle and additive-free' — a quietly principled approach to keeping the soup honest in an era of flavour boosters.
chef_bio.specialty: Shoyu ramen — a light, clear, old-school soy-sauce broth bowl priced around ¥700, prepared without chemical seasoning (無化調). Takana (pickled mustard greens) variations and a ramen-plus-fried-rice lunch set are also available.
chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog places Ryuen in Nagoya Nishi-ku (10-seat counter), describing a 'gentle, additive-free' shoyu broth made without chemical seasoning, takana variations, and a ramen+fried-rice lunch set, in the sub-¥999 price band.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information available on cross-contamination controls. The kitchen is a small counter operation and no allergen protocols are documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard wheat-brewed shoyu is the defining ingredient of the ramen. No wheat-free soy sauce alternative is mentioned in any source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broth is animal-based (chicken and/or pork bone); no plant-only preparation exists at this shop.

cultural_comfort.note: A compact 10-seat counter shop with no English menu or signage; ordering is straightforward but best managed by pointing at the menu or using a translation app.

places.name: 矢場とん 名古屋城金シャチ横丁店 (Yabaton Nagoya Castle Kinshachi Yokocho)
places.gf_detail: Miso katsu is inherently wheat-heavy: the pork cutlet is dredged in wheat flour and coated with panko breadcrumbs before deep-frying. The proprietary miso sauce (aged 18 months, simmered with pork) is soybean-based, but the breading alone makes the core product unsafe for anyone avoiding gluten. No gluten-free preparation, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free menu option has been identified at Yabaton. Celiac diners should not eat here.
places.vegan_detail: Yabaton specializes entirely in breaded pork cutlet (tonkatsu). The core menu is pork-centric with no documented vegan alternatives. Side vegetables and cabbage are present but cooked in the same environment as meat and served alongside animal products. The signature miso sauce is simmered with pork. No vegan menu or plant-based options have been identified at this or any Yabaton location.
places.hours_raw: 10:30–17:30 (L.O. 17:00), follows Nagoya Castle's closure days
places.neighborhood: Naka-ku, Sannomaru (Kinshachi Yokochō, Yoshinao Zone)
places.notes: Yabaton was founded in 1947 by Shigeo Suzuki as 'Yaba no Tonkatsu,' a small shop in front of Minami-Otsu-dori tram stop in postwar Nagoya. Suzuki reportedly discovered the miso katsu concept when he dipped a skewered cutlet into a dote-nabe (miso-braised offal) pot at a street st

chef_bio.background: Yabaton was founded in 1947 by Yoshio Suzuki (鈴木義夫) as 'Yaba no Tonkatsu,' a small shop by the Minami-Otsu-dori 4-chome tram stop in postwar Nagoya. Suzuki reportedly discovered the miso katsu idea when he dipped a skewered cutlet into a dote-nabe (miso-braised offal) pot and was struck by how good it tasted — he then developed his own proprietary miso sauce and built it into a full restaurant. The company was incorporated in 1953, opened its Yabacho flagship in 1970, and passed to second-generation leader Takayuki Suzuki in 1980. Today the chain operates 28 domestic locations and has international outposts including Bangkok. This branch opened inside the Kinshachi Yokocho Yoshinao Zone food district, a curated dining complex built adjacent to Nagoya Castle's main gate.
chef_bio.philosophy: Yabaton insists on dipping the cutlet into miso sauce rather than simply pouring it on top — maintaining the founder's original technique against the modern trend of sauce-on-top service.
chef_bio.specialty: Miso katsu — breaded pork cutlet (loin or fillet) dipped in or plated with a deeply savory red soybean miso sauce made from naturally fermented miso aged around 18 months. The signature 'waraji' (sandal-sized) cutlet is the crowd-draw.

anecdotes.text: The miso katsu idea was born when founder Yoshio Suzuki dipped a kushikatsu (skewered cutlet) into a dote-nabe miso pot and realized the combination was extraordinary — leading him to develop his now-famous proprietary sauce.

A longtime customer recalled: 'During postwar recovery, miso katsu was truly extravagant. We worked hard and enjoyed it only once every few months as our sole pleasure.' It was considered a luxury item in the 1940s and 1950s.

Since 2008, Yabaton has built five elementary schools in Cambodia through in-store donations, and in October 2017 established the 'Yabaton Children's Fund' — unusual community philanthropy for a tonkatsu chain.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Yabaton's official history and Japanese Wikipedia confirm the 1947 founding by Suzuki Yoshio, the dote-nabe dipping origin of miso katsu, 28 domestic branches, the 2014 Bangkok expansion, five Cambodia schools (since 2008) and the 2017 Children's Fund; the Kinshachi Yokocho page confirms the Yoshinao-zone branch and roughly 18-month-aged miso.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All katsu items are deep-fried in shared fryers — seafood (scallop, shrimp, oyster) and vegetable kushikatsu are fried alongside pork cutlets. No dedicated fryer for any item has been documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature dish is coated in wheat flour and panko breadcrumbs before frying — this is the core preparation and cannot be omitted. The miso sauce itself is soybean-based, but cross-contact with wheat throughout the kitchen is guaranteed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All cooking occurs in a pork-focused kitchen. The miso sauce is simmered with pork. No plant-only preparation zone or vegan menu items have been identified.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No specific allergen handling protocols or allergy communication procedures have been documented for this location. The HotPepper listing notes English menus are available but does not mention allergen cards or staff training.

positives.text: Credit cards and multiple e-money formats accepted; English menus available; located in a well-lit tourist-facing complex with clear signage — easier navigation for foreign visitors than a typical street restaurant.

cultural_comfort.note: English menus available; located inside a tourist-facing food complex at Nagoya Castle, so staff are accustomed to international visitors.

places.name: motsu・sen (Motsu Sen)
places.gf_detail: The signature motsusuki is a sukiyaki-style broth almost certainly based on soy sauce (which contains wheat) and mirin. No gluten-free claims or accommodations are mentioned anywhere. Champon noodles (〆) contain wheat. This venue is not safe for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: Entirely meat/offal-focused. Every dish — sashimi of heart and tripe, grilled hormonyaki, the signature motsusuki, champon finish — is built around beef offal. No plant-based or vegan options exist on the menu.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Thu & Sun 17:30–23:00 (food L.O. 22:00, drink L.O. 22:30), Fri/Sat 17:30–24:00 (food L.O. 23:00, drink L.O. 23:30), closed Mon and the 2nd Sunday
places.neighborhood: 那古野・円頓寺横丁 (Nakono / Endoji Yokocho)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened in May 2023 on the second floor of Endoji Yokocho (円頓寺横丁), a refurbished alley of small restaurants in Nagoya's Nishi Ward. The concept was produced by a well-regarded hormonyaki specialist from Shibuya, Tokyo, with the aim of bringing motsu sukiyaki — a style nearly absen

chef_bio.background: Opened in May 2023 on the second floor of Endoji Yokocho (円頓寺横丁), a refurbished alley of small restaurants in Nagoya's Nishi Ward. The concept was produced by a well-regarded hormonyaki specialist from Shibuya, Tokyo, with the aim of bringing motsu sukiyaki — a style nearly absent from Nagoya — to the city in a stylish, accessible setting.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owner/producer insists on daily-fresh offal delivery, personally crafts the original sauces, and sources Cambodian pepper directly — a level of ingredient control unusual for a 16-seat neighbourhood spot. Staff cook tableside to maintain the correct heat and technique throughout the course.
chef_bio.specialty: もつすき (motsusuki): beef offal cooked sukiyaki-style in a soy-based sauce, evolving through three stages from seared offal to full hotpot, finishing with champon noodles dusted with Cambodian pepper sourced directly by the owner.

anecdotes.text: Retty reviewers singled out the motsu sashimi — sliced raw heart and tripe served as course openers — praising its unexpected freshness and refinement.

A ladies-only abbreviated course (6 dishes, ¥3,630) was introduced in early 2024, reflecting the owner's intent to make the venue approachable for diners who might be intimidated by offal-focused omakase.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Nagoyadot and Tabelog confirm a May 2023 opening on the 2F of Endoji Yokocho (Nishi-ku), 16 seats, a Shibuya hormonyaki producer, the three-stage motsusuki with a Cambodian-pepper champon finish, and the ¥3,630 six-dish ladies' course; Retty reviews praise the raw heart and tripe sashimi.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information available on cross-contamination controls. The kitchen handles wheat-containing soy sauce, champon noodles, and likely other wheat-based ingredients throughout service.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Motsusuki is a sukiyaki-style dish whose sauce is almost certainly soy-sauce-based (containing wheat). No gluten-free soy sauce (tamari) alternative is mentioned anywhere in listings or reviews.
The finish course is champon noodles, which contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Entire menu is built around beef offal — no plant-based dishes exist; vegan cross-contact is moot as there is nothing vegan to order.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy-handling policy or allergen menu is referenced in any source. Diners with dietary restrictions should call 052-571-2919 before visiting.

cultural_comfort.note: No English menu or signage mentioned; reservations and communication are in Japanese, though the intimate counter format and staff attentiveness noted in reviews suggest a welcoming environment for those who can gesture or use translation apps.

places.name: 長命うどん 弁天通店 (Chomei Udon Bentendori)
places.gf_detail: Udon is the core product — wheat noodles are unavoidable. Dashi is almost certainly fish-based (bonito/katsuo). Soy sauce in the broth contains wheat. No GF options exist and cross-contamination from wheat is inherent to every dish. Celiac diners cannot eat here safely.
places.vegan_detail: Broth is fish-based dashi (bonito); tempura toppings include seafood. No plant-based or vegan options are offered or noted anywhere in available sources.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Fri 11:00–around 16:00 (closes once sold out), Sat/Sun/holidays 11:00–around 14:00 (closes once sold out), open daily
places.neighborhood: Jōshin (Nishi-ku)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 長命うどん (Chomei Udon) traces its roots to 1913 (Taisho 2), when a Nagoya noodle-stall family was chosen to make the ceremonial first crossing of the newly built Naya Bridge. The city mayor marked the occasion by bestowing the name 'Chomei' (longevity) on the shop. The Bentendori br

chef_bio.origin: Nagoya (Nakamura Ward main shop, est. 1913; Bentendori branch in Nishi Ward)
chef_bio.background: 長命うどん (Chomei Udon) traces its roots to 1913 (Taisho 2), when a Nagoya noodle-stall family was chosen to make the ceremonial first crossing of the newly built Naya Bridge. The city mayor marked the occasion by bestowing the name 'Chomei' (longevity) on the shop. The Bentendori branch is one of several noren-wake (apprentice-spun-off) locations; only trainees who complete an apprenticeship at the main Nakamura-ward shop and are recognised as masters earn the right to open an affiliated branch.
chef_bio.philosophy: Deliver honest, affordable noodles hand-crafted with the same sincerity as the original 1913 shop; slight branch-to-branch variation is accepted as proof of handmade craft.
chef_bio.specialty: Classic Nagoya market-style udon with interchangeable noodle types (udon, soba, chuka soba) and mix-and-match combinations — house-made noodles, light savory broth, kakiage tempura; notably, this branch does not serve kishimen.

anecdotes.text: The name 'Chomei' (long life) was personally granted by the Nagoya city mayor at the Naya Bridge opening ceremony in the Taisho era — an origin story the chain still highlights as its founding legend.

The Bentendori location is housed on the ground floor of an apartment building on the north side of Nagoya Castle's perimeter, with a small rear car park — an incongruous setting that adds to its local-secret feel.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Chomei Udon's official history confirms the 1913 (Taisho 2) Naya Bridge first-crossing and the mayor bestowing the name '長命,' plus the noren-wake apprenticeship-branch system; a local blog confirms the Bentendori branch sits on the ground floor of an apartment north of Nagoya Castle, has rear parking, and does not serve kishimen.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat is the primary ingredient (udon noodles); cross-contamination is inherent and unavoidable throughout the kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Broth is seasoned with standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce alternatives are mentioned in any source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Fish-based dashi is used in the broth; tempura includes seafood toppings. No plant-based broth option is available.

cultural_comfort.note: A no-frills cash-only counter shop with no English menu or signage; basic Japanese is helpful for ordering, though the short menu and pointing at photos or the menu board is manageable.

places.name: もつ藤 (Motsu Fuji)
places.gf_detail: The motsunabe broth uses a house-made soy-sauce-based vinegar tare, and soy sauce normally contains wheat. The finishing noodles (champon, sesame ramen) also contain wheat. There is no explicit gluten-free accommodation, and food-allergy handling must be confirmed by phone in advance. Whether a dedicated fryer exists is also unknown.
places.vegan_detail: A meat-focused specialty restaurant whose signature dish is motsunabe (offal). There are no plant-only options, and vegan accommodation is not possible.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Wed, Thu, Sun 17:00–23:00 (L.O. 22:00); Fri, Sat 17:00–24:00 (L.O. 23:00); closed Tue
places.neighborhood: Marunouchi, Naka Ward / City Hall & Hisaya-ōdōri Station area
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] From Ichinomiya, Aichi Prefecture. He quit his office job to attend culinary school, trained at a Chinese restaurant, and further honed his skills at a gyoza specialty shop in Osaka. He then trained for about a year and a half at the Osaka branch of a prestigious Fukuoka motsunabe restaurant, and in 2007 opened "Motsu Fuji" in Marunouchi, Nagoya, as a franchise offshoot (noren-wake).

chef_bio.origin: 福岡・博多の名門もつ鍋から暖簾分け（2007年開業）
chef_bio.background: 愛知県一宮市出身。会社員を辞めて調理師専門学校へ進み、中華料理店での修業を経て大阪の餃子専門店でも腕を磨いた。その後、福岡の名門もつ鍋店が大阪に出した支店で約1年半の修業を積み、2007年に暖簾分けの形で名古屋・丸の内に「もつ藤」を開いた。
chef_bio.philosophy: 国産の新鮮な食材と九州の本場調味料にこだわり、くどさのないすっきりとした味わいを追求。カウンターでは焼き物を目の前で仕上げるライブ感も楽しめる。
chef_bio.specialty: 博多水炊き風もつ鍋。丁寧に下茹で・脂抜きした国産モツを、自家製の酢だれやゆずこしょうで好みに味付けして食べるあっさりとした一鍋。

anecdotes.text: もつ鍋の上に餃子の皮を蓋のようにのせ、そのまま鍋に溶かし込むのが看板の食べ方。〆はちゃんぽんかごまラーメンで、テーブルごしに大量のごまをかけるパフォーマンスも名物のひとつ。
伊藤シェフは「餃子を焼くとき、普通の水の代わりに熱湯を使うと温度が下がらずパリッと仕上がる」と語る。

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Hitosara confirms owner-chef Ito Kazuyuki (from Ichinomiya), his culinary-school -> Chinese-restaurant -> Osaka gyoza -> Fukuoka motsunabe (about 1.5 years) path, the 2007 Marunouchi noren-wake opening, the Hakata mizutaki-style motsunabe with a gyoza-skin lid and goma-ramen/champon finish, and his hot-water gyoza tip; nagoya-meshi confirms the tableside grilling and the black-sesame performance.

gf_cross_contamination.text: グルテンフリー対応や交差汚染への取り組みについて公式・口コミ上での言及はなく、詳細は不明。事前の電話確認が必須。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: もつ鍋の自家製たれに醤油ベースの酢だれを使用。一般的な醤油には小麦が含まれるため、セリアック病や小麦アレルギーの方は要注意。グルテンフリー醤油使用の記載なし。

vegan_cross_contact.text: もつ鍋専門店であり、全メニューが動物性食材を使用。ビーガン・ベジタリアン向けの選択肢および交差汚染への配慮についての情報はない。

staff_allergy_handling.text: アレルギー対応や食事制限への配慮に関する記載はウェブ上に見当たらない。来店前に電話（052-961-9525）で確認することを強く推奨。

cultural_comfort.note: 地元客中心の小さな居酒屋で、英語メニューや英語対応スタッフの記載はなく、日本語でのやり取りが基本となる。

places.name: 晴快荘 (Seikaisou)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free claims or allergen information published. Wheat risk is high on multiple fronts: soy sauce (tamari not mentioned) is standard in Japanese izakaya cooking and almost certainly used in simmered fish and dipping sauces; the lunch menu explicitly features フライ定食 (fried dishes), meaning wheat-battered items are fried in the kitchen; dashi-based broths may contain wheat-containing seasonings. No dedicated fryer. The restaurant has not addressed cross-contamination. Celiac diners should avoid.
places.vegan_detail: A seafood-specialist izakaya built around dashi-based broths (the signature mozuku tako shabu uses seafood stock), fish sashimi, simmered fish, and seasonal fugu courses. No plant-based or vegan options are indicated anywhere on the menu or website. Hidden dashi (bonito/seafood) is present in virtually every dish.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Fri 11:30–13:00 / 17:00–22:00; Sat 17:00–22:00; closed Sun and holidays
places.neighborhood: Marunouchi / 5 min walk from Nagoyajō Station
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Seikaisou is a seafood restaurant opened in Nagoya by a family that runs the ryokan "Hotel Seikaisou" on Himakajima Island, Aichi Prefecture. With the wish that guests "casually enjoy the island's bounty," it serves torafugu, octopus, and seasonal seafood shipped directly from Himakajima. The main location is in the basement (B1) of the Aichi Fisheries Hall — a place tied to the fishing industry — and it has been loved by local businesspeople and gourmets since the 1970s.

chef_bio.background: 晴快荘は愛知県日間賀島で旅館「ホテル晴快荘」を営む一家が名古屋に開いた海鮮料理店。「島の幸を気軽に楽しんでほしい」という思いのもと、日間賀島産のとらふぐやタコ、季節の魚介を産地直送で提供する。本店は愛知県水産会館の地下1階（名古屋市中区丸の内）という漁業ゆかりの場所に構え、1970年（開業）から地元ビジネスパーソンや食通に愛され続けている。
chef_bio.philosophy: 日間賀島から直送した旬の食材を「お値打ちな価格でくつろいだ雰囲気で」提供することをモットーとする。
chef_bio.specialty: もずくたこしゃぶ（日間賀島産タコを香り高い出汁ともずく・ワカメと合わせる名物しゃぶしゃぶ）、季節のとらふぐコース（10月〜3月）

anecdotes.text: レビュアーや公式サイトは女将を日間賀島生まれの「気さくな女将」と評し、カジュアルで温かみのある接客が常連客を引きつけていると記している。
ランチも人気で、丸の内界隈の地元ビジネスパーソンや常連客に支持されている。

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog and the official site confirm Seikaisou opened in 1970, is run by a Himakajima-born proprietress whose family runs the Hotel Seikaisou ryokan, sits in the B1 of the Aichi Fisheries Hall (Marunouchi 3-4-31), and features mozuku tako-shabu (created by the second-generation owner) and October-March torafugu courses.

gf_cross_contamination.text: ランチメニューにフライ定食（揚げ物）が含まれており、同一キッチンで小麦を使用した揚げ物が調理されている。専用フライヤーの有無は不明だが、小麦との接触汚染リスクは高い。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: 醤油ベースの味付けが標準的な居酒屋・海鮮料理店であり、煮魚・タレ類に小麦を含む通常の醤油が使用されていると考えられる。グルテンフリー醤油（タマリ等）の使用は確認されていない。
