places.vegan_detail: All broths are built on pork bone (tonkotsu) and whole chicken stock, combined with bonito, horse mackerel, and mackerel dashi. No plant-based broth or vegan menu items are offered. The Taiwan mazesoba also uses meat-based seasoning.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–15:00 (LO 14:30), 17:00–22:00 (LO 21:00); hours may shorten on certain dates — verify with venue
places.neighborhood: Nagoya Castle, Kinshachi Yokochō Muneharu Zone, Shiyakusho Stn
places.notes: Ryuichiro Sawa was born in Nagoya in 1971. After five years in the corporate sector, he pursued his passion for ramen by opening Chuuka Soba Tsurumaiikkokkuya, which drew lines from its very first month. While researching ramen across Japan, he encountered tsukemen — then a Tokyo

chef_bio.origin: 名古屋市大須 (Nagoya, Osu district), 2009
chef_bio.background: Ryuichiro Sawa was born in Nagoya in 1971. After five years as a company employee, he pursued his passion for ramen — opening Chuuka Soba Tsurumaiikkokkuya in Tsurumai, which drew lines from its very first month. While researching ramen across Japan, he encountered tsukemen — then a Tokyo phenomenon — and set out to bring it to Nagoya. He founded Fujiyama55 at its Osu flagship in 2009, widely credited with igniting Nagoya's concentrated-tsukemen boom and dispelling the city's reputation as a ramen backwater.
chef_bio.philosophy: Sawa's guiding idea: 'I want to do fun things with my team — work itself is the highest form of entertainment.' Noodles are hand-crafted using Aichi-brand wheat (きぬあかり/ゆめあかり), kansui, and potato starch, with the ratio adjusted for temperature and humidity.
chef_bio.specialty: 魚介濃厚つけ麺 — thick house-made wheat noodles served alongside a concentrated tonkotsu-torigara broth enriched with fish dashi (bonito, horse mackerel, mackerel); the Kinshachi Yokocho branch also features Nagoya miso ramen and Taiwan mazesoba

anecdotes.text: Sawa consumes over 500 bowls of ramen per year as part of his ongoing research into flavor.

The Fujiyama55 name reflects a twin ambition: Mount Fuji-inspired generous portions (フジヤマ盛) and the '55 (go-go)' energy of firing up the brand — which now runs 59 locations (43 in Japan, 16 overseas) across Japan, Taiwan, China, Hong Kong, Thailand, Indonesia, and France.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Fujiyama55's official site and the 本店の旅 profile confirm founder Sawa Ryuichiro (b.1971, Nagoya), five years as a company employee, the 2003 Tsurumai ramen shop, the 2009 founding of Fujiyama55 at its Osu flagship, the 富士山盛 + 55(GOGO) name origin, Aichi wheat (kinuakari/yumeakari), 500+ bowls/year, and 59 stores (43 domestic / 16 overseas).

gf_cross_contamination.text: The entire kitchen is built around wheat-based noodles made fresh on-site daily; cross-contamination with gluten is certain and unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Broths combine pork bone, chicken, and fish dashi; standard Japanese soy sauce (containing wheat) is almost certainly used in seasoning tare — no wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned anywhere.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Every broth on the menu contains pork bone, chicken stock, and/or fish dashi (bonito, mackerel, horse mackerel). There is no plant-based alternative and shared cooking equipment makes vegan cross-contact unavoidable.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy menu, allergy statement, or staff allergy protocol is published by the restaurant or venue. Diners with serious allergies should call ahead: 052-212-7375.

positives.text: Noodles are made with only three ingredients (wheat flour, kansui, potato starch) and zero preservatives or additives, which simplifies the ingredient picture even if it does not remove wheat.

cultural_comfort.note: Counter-heavy ramen shop inside a tourist-oriented food hall next to Nagoya Castle; staff interaction is minimal and ordering is straightforward, but menus and signage are primarily in Japanese.

places.name: 贅食家千壽 丸の内店 (Zeishokuya Senju Marunouchi)
places.gf_detail: Standard wheat-containing soy sauce (醤油) is used for sushi and sashimi — the default in Japanese sushi restaurants. Dashi is seafood-based. No gluten-free soy sauce, tamari, or dedicated GF preparations were found or advertised. Tempura appears on the parent location's menu, indicating shared-kitchen wheat use. Cross-contamination risk is real. Diners with celiac disease should contact the restaurant directly before visiting; do not assume safety.
places.vegan_detail: This is a traditional sushi and seafood izakaya. Miso soup uses seafood-based dashi (海鮮出汁). Fish, shellfish, and animal-derived broths are core to the menu. No plant-based or vegan alternatives were found.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:00–15:00, dinner 17:00–20:00 (daily). Regular closing day unknown (may have irregular days off).
places.neighborhood: Marunouchi, Naka Ward (~6 min walk from Marunouchi Station)
places.notes: Zeishokuya Senju is a sushi izakaya headquartered in Kurokawa, Kita Ward, Nagoya; the Marunouchi branch newly opened on April 5, 2026. At the main location, owner Toshiyuki Kameda personally buys whole fish at the northern market and tastes them to verify quality.

chef_bio.chef_name: 亀田俊幸 (Kameda Toshiyuki) — 本店
chef_bio.origin: 名古屋市北区田幡（黒川エリア／本店）、丸の内店は2026年4月開業
chef_bio.background: 贅食家千壽は名古屋市北区田幡（黒川エリア）に本店を構える寿司居酒屋で、丸の内店（名古屋市中区丸の内）は2026年4月5日にグランドオープンした支店。本店では店主の亀田俊幸氏が北部市場で魚を丸ごと一本自ら仕入れ、試食して品質を確認するスタイルを貫いている。
chef_bio.philosophy: 「贅沢をお値打ちに」——高品質な寿司を手の届く価格で提供することを使命とし、値上げをせずに赤字覚悟で運営を続ける姿勢が話題を呼んでいる。
chef_bio.specialty: にぎり寿司（1貫160円からの圧倒的コスパ）、あたりまえランチ（¥1,990で10貫のにぎりとA5飛騨牛）

anecdotes.text: 本店はテレビ愛知で特集放送後に売上が約2倍に。店主は設備増強のために最大限の融資を受けたにもかかわらず、価格を据え置き続けている。

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: テレビ愛知（yomu.tv-aichi）が店主・亀田俊幸氏、北区田幡の本店、北部市場での一本買いと試食、値上げせず赤字覚悟の姿勢、1貫160円・¥1,990あたりまえランチ（10貫＋A5飛騨牛）を確認。Tabelogが丸の内店（中区丸の内）の2026年4月5日グランドオープンを確認。

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information on dedicated fryers or separation of wheat-containing preparations. The parent menu includes tempura, indicating wheat flour is used in the kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce (wheat-containing) is the default at sushi restaurants. No gluten-free soy sauce or tamari was mentioned anywhere in available sources.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Seafood-based dashi (海鮮出汁) is used in miso soup. Fish and shellfish are prepared throughout the kitchen. No plant-based separation is available.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy menu, allergen labeling, or staff allergy-handling protocol was found in any source. Celiac diners should call ahead: 052-201-3655.

positives.text: Counter-style seating means you can communicate directly with the chef about what goes into each piece, which may allow some customization.

cultural_comfort.note: The restaurant is a casual sushi counter with no English-language presence found; basic Japanese will go a long way, though counter sushi shops are generally navigable by pointing at items.

places.name: あんかけスパゲティ NANAYA (Ankake Spaghetti NANAYA)
places.gf_detail: The core dish is wheat spaghetti — gluten is structural, not incidental. The sauce may also contain soy sauce (wheat-bearing) as a seasoning. No gluten-free pasta or allergen-accommodation is mentioned in any source. This restaurant is not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: Ankake spaghetti is a wheat-pasta dish fried in lard with a thick tomato-pepper sauce; standard toppings include wieners, bacon, and egg. No plant-based or vegan options are indicated anywhere on the menu or in reviews.
places.neighborhood: Marunouchi / Naka-ku, Nagoya
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] NANAYA opened on 24 October 2022 in a ground-floor space at Sun Mansion Marunouchi, a short walk from Marunouchi Station. Reviews note it is a compact, counter-led shop described as run by a kind couple, though no further owner details are publicly documented. A Tabelog review me

chef_bio.background: NANAYA opened on 24 October 2022 in a ground-floor space at Sun Mansion Marunouchi, about a 2-minute walk from Marunouchi Station. Reviews describe it as a compact, counter-led neighbourhood shop; no owner details are publicly documented.
chef_bio.specialty: Ankake spaghetti — the classic Nagoya-born dish of thick wheat noodles stir-fried and blanketed in a spicy, starch-thickened tomato-pepper sauce. NANAYA's version is praised for its punchy, peppery sauce and satisfying chew. Its most popular order is the Mirakan (ミラカン), the standard ankake style topped with sausage plus sautéed onions and peppers; cutlet toppings are also offered.
chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: kaiten-heiten confirms the 24 Oct 2022 opening at Sun Mansion Marunouchi 1F, Naka-ku, near Marunouchi Station. Uber Eats / Tabelog menu listings confirm the Mirakan (sausage + onion/pepper mix) as the top item (¥1,690) and cutlet toppings. Owner not publicly named.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The restaurant's core product is wheat spaghetti; the kitchen works exclusively with wheat pasta and there is no indication of any gluten-free preparation area or protocols.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Ankake spaghetti sauce may contain soy sauce, which in Japan standardly contains wheat. No source confirms NANAYA uses tamari or any gluten-free soy sauce alternative.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Noodles are fried in lard and standard toppings include wieners and egg; no plant-only preparation line exists.

cultural_comfort.note: Small 18-seat counter-focused shop with no English menu noted; ordering is manageable by pointing at the menu, but staff are unlikely to speak English.

places.name: 創作串揚げ つだ 金シャチ横丁店 (Sosaku Kushiage Tsuda Kinshachi Yokocho)
places.gf_detail: Kushiage is deep-fried in wheat-flour batter (パン粉 breadcrumbs over a flour-egg-based coat) — standard wheat throughout. Frying oil is a lard-and-vegetable-oil blend used for all items; there is no dedicated GF fryer. Dipping options include a blended soy sauce (合わせ醤油) that almost certainly contains wheat, plus salt, lemon, and sansho salt which are likely safe. The restaurant states food-allergy accommodation is available (フードアレルギー対応可), so it is worth calling ahead, but celiac-safe dining cannot be assumed without direct confirmation.
places.vegan_detail: No dedicated vegan menu. Courses are heavily meat- and seafood-focused (wagyu, live prawn, hamo, scallop, salmon roe). Vegetable skewers do appear in courses (asparagus, green pea croquette, ginkgo nuts), but they share oil with meat/seafood, the batter very likely contains eggs, and dipping sauces include a blended soy sauce and a house sauce whose exact ingredients are unlisted. Hidden dashi (katsuo/bonito) is possible in sauces. Vegan guests should call ahead and ask explicitly — ad-hoc accommodation is noted as possible (フードアレルギー対応可) but a fully vegan course is not offered.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu–Sun & holidays 11:00–15:00 (L.O. 14:00) / 17:00–22:00 (L.O. 21:00), closed Wed (some Wednesdays open in April)
places.neighborhood: Naka-ku, Ninomaru (Kinshachi Yokochō, Muneharu Zone)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Tsuda Takeshi graduated from an Osaka culinary school and spent five years mastering French cuisine before a single meal at Rokakutei — a Michelin one-star kushikatsu specialist with locations in Osaka and Tokyo — changed his direction entirely. He joined Rokakutei and spent a de

chef_bio.background: Tsuda Takeshi graduated from an Osaka culinary school and spent five years mastering French cuisine before a single meal at Rokakutei — a Michelin one-star kushikatsu specialist with locations in Osaka and Tokyo — changed his direction entirely. He joined Rokakutei and spent a decade there as its lead fryer, absorbing the techniques that would earn the restaurant its star. In March 2013, after 25 years in the craft, he opened his own flagship in Nagoya's Higashi Ward. The Kinshachi Yokocho branch opened on 29 March 2018, the same month Nagoya Castle's gourmet street debuted, and a Tokyo outpost at Toranomon Hills followed in 2024.
chef_bio.philosophy: Tsuda's guiding principle is that the ingredient is the star, not the batter. His coating is deliberately as thin as possible — built up with meringue and milk for lightness, finished with very fine breadcrumbs — and fried slowly at 170°C to coax natural flavour forward. He treats kushiage as washoku, using only sushi-grade ingredients that could in principle be eaten raw, and serves each skewer at the moment it is perfect.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal omakase kushiage course — live tiger prawn, Omi beef Tomosankaku, Aomori scallop, hamo with cream cheese, and salmon-with-ikura ('parent-and-child skewer') are signature items

anecdotes.text: Tsuda has said he was shocked when he first ate at Rokakutei: he had assumed kushiage was simply 'put batter on, fry, dip in sauce' — the experience upended that assumption and sent him on a 25-year journey to elevate the form.
A secret menu item — fried-rice-cracker tea rice, originally made for the owner's mother — became so popular with customers it was eventually added to the standard course.
Tsuda has publicly stated his next ambitions: Tokyo recognition, then a potential New York outpost by age 64, positioning creative kushiage as a global fine-dining form.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Sangyo-Times confirms the Osaka culinary school, 5 years of French cuisine, the 10-year Rokakutei apprenticeship, kushiage started at 24 (~30 yrs by 2024), the 2013 Nagoya / 2018 Kinshachi / Jan-2024 Toranomon Hills openings, washoku/sushi-grade philosophy and the NY-by-64 ambition. Savor Japan confirms the meringue+milk 170°C batter and the tiger-prawn, Omi-beef, Aomori-scallop, hamo-cream-cheese and salmon/ikura signature skewers.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All kushiage items share the same lard-and-vegetable-oil frying blend; there is no separate dedicated GF fryer. Wheat from standard batter will be present in the oil throughout service.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The house dipping options include a blended soy sauce (合わせ醤油, described as 30+ ingredient base) which in standard Japanese practice contains wheat. Salt, sansho salt, lemon, and mustard options exist and are likely wheat-free, but confirmation is required.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All skewers are fried in the same shared oil (lard-and-vegetable blend). Lard is animal-derived. Even vegetable skewers are cooked in lard-containing oil alongside meat and seafood items.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Hitosara lists フードアレルギー対応可 (food-allergy accommodation available) for this location. The nature and extent of accommodation is not further specified; direct inquiry before visiting is strongly recommended.

positives.text: The restaurant uses exclusively domestic, sushi-grade ingredients and communicates openly about provenance — the counter format allows direct conversation with the chef about what is in each skewer.

cultural_comfort.note: Counter-style omakase dining with course service; English is not widely spoken, but online reservations are available and the format is intuitive for non-Japanese speakers.

places.name: 名古屋飯店 (Nagoya Hanten)
places.gf_detail: Standard machi-chuka kitchen — soy sauce (wheat-containing) is a baseline ingredient across virtually every dish including the signature ketchup-rice omurice. No gluten-free options, dedicated fryer, or allergen accommodation mentioned on Tabelog, the official Instagram, or any reviewed source. Wheat exposure is near-certain; not safe for celiac or serious gluten intolerance.
places.vegan_detail: Signature omurice is built on eggs and likely pork-based fried rice; other menu items include pork, meat-based broths, and lard-fried dishes typical of machi-chuka style. No plant-based options identified.
places.neighborhood: Kaminagoya, Nishi Ward — 4-minute walk north of Joshin Stati
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Noma-san spent his working life as an electrical equipment salesman before pivoting to professional cooking at age 40 — a late-start story that became a local talking point. He trained at several Chinese restaurants, including running Taka-hanten in the Endonji shotengai, before

chef_bio.background: Noma-san, originally from Kyushu, spent his working life as an electrical equipment salesman before pivoting to professional cooking at age 40 — a late-start story that became a local talking point. He trained at several Chinese restaurants, including running Taka-hanten (タカ飯店) in the Endonji shotengai, before opening Nagoya Hanten on 13 December 2025.
chef_bio.philosophy: Noma's stated goal was to make an omurice that could only come from a Chinese restaurant — combining the technique of wok-fried rice with the silky egg work of tenshin-don. He refuses to compromise on ingredients despite a cost ratio around 50%.
chef_bio.specialty: 町中華本気のオムライス (machi-chuka honki no omurice) — ketchup fried rice sautéed in a wok, blanketed in a soft, barely-set sheet of premium Miyazaki 康卵 eggs (about ¥100 per egg, five per plate).

anecdotes.text: Despite each premium Miyazaki 康卵 egg costing around ¥100, Noma uses over 200 eggs a day and five per omurice, and doesn't blink at the food-cost math — the right egg, he says, is what makes the dish taste right.
The rice is Akita Komachi from Akita Prefecture, chosen for its firmness and mild sweetness when stir-fried — Noma considers it non-negotiable for both the fried rice and the omurice base.
The shop quickly became a daily-queue destination after opening in December 2025, with the machi-chuka omurice accounting for the large majority of orders.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Yahoo!ニュースが店主（元・電気関係の営業マン、九州出身）、宮崎産『康卵』（約100円/個）、原価率約50%、Akita Komachi、連日の行列を確認。jouhou.nagoyaが2025年12月13日開業・40歳から料理人・オムライス1皿に康卵5個を、oinagoyaが円頓寺商店街でタカ飯店を営んでいた経歴を確認。

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information on cross-contamination controls available; standard machi-chuka kitchen implies shared woks and utensils used across all dishes.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Wheat-containing soy sauce is a foundational ingredient in Chinese restaurant cooking and almost certainly used across sauces, marinades, and stir-fry bases here; no tamari or GF soy sauce alternative mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Shared wok cooking with meat, pork mince (Taiwan dishes), and animal-based stocks makes vegan cross-contact unavoidable.

cultural_comfort.note: QR-code ordering system for meals; Japanese-only menu and signage, but the ordering flow is straightforward and staff interaction is minimal.

places.name: ひつまぶし名古屋備長 金シャチ横丁店 (Hitsumabushi Nagoya Bincho Kinshachi Yokocho)
places.gf_detail: The house tare (sauce) is made with tamari soy sauce, mirin, and sugar. Traditional tamari contains little or no wheat compared to standard koikuchi soy sauce, but Bincho has not publicly confirmed their tamari is wheat-free or certified gluten-free. The eel itself is grilled over binchotan charcoal with no breading. The ochazuke broth (dashi) does not contain wheat but is fish-based. No dedicated GF menu or official allergy accommodation policy is published. Celiac diners should contact the restaurant directly before visiting.
places.vegan_detail: Eel is the sole focus of the menu. The signature ochazuke eating method uses fish-based dashi broth. No plant-based alternatives exist. Not suitable for vegans.
places.hours_raw: Weekdays 10:30–17:30 (L.O. 16:00), Sat/Sun/holidays 10:30–15:30 (L.O. 15:00), 16:30–19:00 (L.O. 18:30). Closed Mon (if Mon is a holiday, next day). Follows Nagoya Castle's closure days. Closes once eel sells out.
places.neighborhood: Naka-ku, Sannomaru (Kinshachi Yokochō)
places.notes: Bincho Co., Ltd. was founded in September 1992 by Hiroshi Suzuki, a native of Aichi Prefecture. After completing a decade-long apprenticeship at Unagi no Nishimoto, a longstanding Nagoya eel establishment, Suzuki opened the first Bincho location. The company name derives from bin

chef_bio.origin: Aichi Prefecture, Japan (founded 1992; now 12 locations across Aichi, Tokyo, Osaka, and Fukuoka)
chef_bio.background: Bincho Co., Ltd. (株式会社備長) was founded in September 1992 by Hiroshi Suzuki. Suzuki first trained for a year at an eel restaurant in Yanagawa, Fukuoka, then completed a decade-long apprenticeship at Unagi no Nishimoto, a longstanding Nagoya eel establishment, before opening the first Bincho location in 1992. The company name derives from binchotan (備長炭), the white oak charcoal central to the restaurant's cooking method.
chef_bio.philosophy: Freshly grilled eel (焼きたて) paired with freshly cooked rice (炊きたて): eel is grilled only after each order, and rice is cooked in small batches to guarantee peak texture. The brand's mission is preserving and disseminating the traditional culture of eel cuisine (鰻の伝統文化の伝承).
chef_bio.specialty: Hitsumabushi — charcoal-grilled eel (unagi) served over rice in a wooden ohitsu vessel, eaten three ways: plain, with condiments (wasabi, negi, nori), and poured with dashi broth as ochazuke.

anecdotes.text: Suzuki learned Nagoya's distinctive jiyaki (地焼き) technique — direct charcoal grilling without the steaming step used in Kanto-style eel — during his 10-year apprenticeship at Unagi no Nishimoto. The method renders fat from the skin, yielding the restaurant's signature crispy exterior and tender interior.

The Kinshachi Yokocho branch sits in the Yoshinao Zone (義直ゾーン) of the castle-side food district, and the menu includes a location-exclusive 金シャチひつまぶし (Gold Shachi Hitsumabushi) — a nod to the golden shachihoko ornaments atop Nagoya Castle.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 備長の公式会社案内が1992年9月創業・創業者鈴木博・柳川(福岡)で1年→名古屋『うなぎの西本』で10年修業・3ブランド12店舗(愛知/東京/大阪/福岡)・『鰻の伝統文化の伝承』を確認。金シャチ横丁インタビューが蒸さない地焼き技法を、店舗ページが金シャチ横丁店の義直ゾーン立地と金シャチひつまぶし(¥7,650)を確認。

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information is published about shared cooking surfaces or cross-contamination protocols. The open charcoal grill environment means multiple items may share preparation space.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The house tare uses tamari soy sauce (たまり醤油), which is traditionally brewed with little or no wheat — unlike standard koikuchi soy sauce. However, Bincho has not publicly confirmed the specific tamari is wheat-free or carries any GF certification. Celiac diners must verify directly.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Not applicable — no vegan dishes on the menu.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy menu or published accommodation policy found across the official website, Kinshachi Yokocho listing, Nagoya tourism info, or third-party reservation platforms. Phone reservations are available (052-223-5777) and contacting the restaurant in advance is the only recommended path for allergy concerns.

positives.text: Core protein is plain eel, charcoal-grilled with no breading or tempura coating — minimal ingredient complexity compared to multi-component Japanese dishes.

cultural_comfort.note: Located in the tourist-oriented Kinshachi Yokocho district next to Nagoya Castle; staff are accustomed to international visitors but the menu and ordering experience remain primarily Japanese-language.

places.name: 焼肉 だいいち (Yakiniku Daiichi)
places.gf_detail: Standard yakiniku tare (dipping sauce) and marinades in Japan typically contain wheat-based soy sauce. No gluten-free menu or allergen information is listed anywhere on Tabelog or the restaurant's Instagram. Dedicated GF accommodation is unconfirmed — those with celiac disease should enquire before booking about whether tamari or wheat-free alternatives are available. Cross-contamination risk from shared grilling surfaces is a further consideration.
places.vegan_detail: This is a meat-focused yakiniku and horumon (offal) restaurant with course menus built around black wagyu beef. No plant-based or vegan options are offered.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sat, day before holidays & day after holidays 18:00–23:00 (food L.O. 22:30) / closed Sun & holidays
places.neighborhood: Josai, Nishi Ward — approx. 500m southwest of Nagoya Castle
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened in October 2025 in a compact 16-seat space on the first floor of Heights SS building in Josai, a quiet residential street a short walk southwest of Nagoya Castle. The restaurant operates on a fully reservation-only basis with online booking only.

chef_bio.background: Opened in October 2025 in a compact 16-seat space on the first floor of Heights SS building in Josai, a quiet residential street a short walk southwest of Nagoya Castle. The restaurant operates on a fully reservation-only basis with online booking only.
chef_bio.specialty: Signature wagyu course centred on black wagyu beef (黒毛和牛); the restaurant claims to be the originator of miso-tan (みそたん発祥の店), a miso-marinated beef tongue preparation.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's Instagram identifies it as the birthplace of miso-tan (みそたん発祥の店), a distinctive preparation of beef tongue finished with miso.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Tabelog confirms the Oct 6 2025 opening, 16 seats, the Nishi-ku Josai / Heights SS Bldg 1F address, fully reservation-only booking, the ¥8,800 black-wagyu course, and 名物『みそたん』. The 発祥(originator) claim comes from the shop's own Instagram and is attributed as such. Owner not publicly named.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Shared charcoal grills are standard at yakiniku restaurants; no information about dedicated or separated grilling equipment for allergen-free cooking.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Yakiniku tare (dipping sauces) and marinades in Japan are typically made with wheat-containing soy sauce. No gluten-free soy sauce (tamari) alternative is mentioned anywhere in the restaurant's listings.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Entirely meat-focused menu with no plant-based dishes; vegan cross-contact is not applicable as vegan dining is not possible here.

cultural_comfort.note: Fully reservation-only with online booking only (no phone calls accepted); signage and communication are in Japanese, so comfort with Japanese-language booking platforms is needed.

places.name: 天ぷら かき揚げ 光村 (Tempura Kakiage Mitsumura)
places.gf_detail: Traditional tempura is made with wheat-flour batter; no gluten-free batter or dedicated fryer documented. Soy-based dipping sauce (tentsuyu) almost certainly contains wheat-based soy sauce. Sesame oil is used for frying, but the frying oil is shared across all tempura and kakiage. No allergen menu, no staff allergy protocol, and no tamari or GF alternatives are mentioned on the official site or any reviewed source. Celiac diners should avoid.
places.vegan_detail: The kitchen centers on shrimp-heavy kakiage and other seafood tempura. Even vegetable tempura items are fried in the same oil as shrimp and fish. Dipping sauce (tentsuyu) is typically made with dashi from bonito/fish. No vegan-safe option or plant-based dashi confirmed. Vegans cannot safely eat here without direct confirmation from staff, which the restaurant does not facilitate (no English phone support confirmed).
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue (2nd and 4th), Thu–Sun 11:00–14:00 (L.O. 13:30) and 17:00–21:00 (L.O. 20:20). Closed Wed and the 1st and 3rd Tue of each month. Closes once ingredients run out.
places.neighborhood: Shimizu, Kita Ward (near Shimizu Station)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Mitsumura is a tempura specialty shop founded in 1973 (Shōwa 48). Rooted in a residential area of Shimizu, Kita Ward, Nagoya, it is a long-established shop that has been loved by locals for over half a century. It earned a Michelin Plate in the 2019 Michelin Guide Aichi-Gifu-Mie edition and was selected for the Tabelog Tempura Top 100 in 2025.

chef_bio.origin: 名古屋市北区清水（1973年創業）
chef_bio.background: 光村は1973年（昭和48年）創業の天ぷら専門店。名古屋市北区清水の住宅街に根を張り、半世紀以上にわたって地元客に愛されてきた老舗。
chef_bio.philosophy: 食材がなくなり次第終了という営業スタイルが示すとおり、鮮度と素材本位を重視する姿勢を一貫している。
chef_bio.specialty: かき揚げ丼。ぷりぷりの小海老をふんだんに使ったかき揚げは丼から溢れんばかりの肉厚さで、甘辛のたれとごま油の香りが特徴。特上かき揚げ丼は海老の量が約2倍。

anecdotes.text: 昼の開店前から行列ができるほどの人気店で、テーブルの入店順ではなく注文順に料理が提供されるという独自のシステムを採用している。
半世紀続く北区の天ぷらカウンターは『2019年版ミシュランガイド愛知・岐阜・三重』でミシュランプレートに掲載され、材料がなくなり次第閉店する素材本位の姿勢が評価されている。

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: jouhou.nagoyaが昭和48年(1973)創業・北区清水・かき揚げ丼(特上は海老約2倍)・入店順ではなく注文順の提供・材料がなくなり次第終了・2019年ミシュランプレート掲載を確認。tp-mitsumura公式サイトが北区清水3-19-18の所在と『材料がなくなり次第終了』を確認。

gf_cross_contamination.text: All tempura and kakiage — including vegetable items — are fried in the same sesame oil. No dedicated fryer for any allergen category is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tentsuyu dipping sauce and bowl tare are standard Japanese preparations that almost certainly contain wheat-based soy sauce; no tamari or GF soy sauce alternative is mentioned anywhere.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegetable tempura items share the frying oil with shrimp and fish tempura; no separation of cooking surfaces or oil confirmed.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen menu, written allergy protocol, or English-language inquiry pathway has been documented on the official site or any third-party source reviewed.

positives.text: Sesame oil is used for frying (not blended vegetable shortening), which some diners prefer for its distinct flavour profile and lower prevalence of certain seed allergens.

cultural_comfort.note: The restaurant is a local neighbourhood institution; an English menu download is available on the official site, but the setting, ordering process, and staff interaction are primarily in Japanese.

places.name: 郷土素材料理 山人昔楼 (Sanjin Mukashiro)
places.gf_detail: Wheat is pervasive: soy sauce and miso appear throughout, tempura batter is used in multiple dishes, and rice/noodle courses include soy-glazed preparations. No gluten-free menu or dedicated GF accommodation is advertised. The restaurant states allergies can be addressed with advance notice, but there is no evidence of celiac-safe protocols or a dedicated fryer. Celiacs should contact the restaurant directly before booking and ask specifically about wheat-free alternatives to soy sauce (tamari) and tempura dishes.
places.vegan_detail: Standard kappo menu built around meat, fish, and seafood. Nearly all dishes incorporate fish-based dashi, soy sauce, or miso. No dedicated vegan options are listed. Allergy accommodation is possible with advance notice, but vegan requests would require explicit communication at booking time — not guaranteed.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Fri 17:00–22:00 (L.O. 21:00), Sat 11:30–14:30 (L.O. 13:30), 17:30–22:00 (L.O. 21:00), Sun 11:30–14:30 (L.O. 13:30), closed Mon. Lunch only on Sat/Sun (weekday lunch by reservation of 10+ people only).
places.neighborhood: Higashi-ku, Shirakabe / Amagasaka
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A 70-year-old traditional private residence adjacent to a small park in Nagoya's Shirakabe district was carefully renovated and opened as a restaurant in November 2005. The building underwent a further major renovation and reopened in October 2025. The location is a 1-minute walk

chef_bio.background: A 70-year-old traditional private residence adjacent to a small park in Nagoya's Shirakabe district was carefully renovated and opened as a restaurant in November 2005. The building underwent a further major renovation and reopened in October 2025. The location is a 1-minute walk from Amagasaka Station, tucked into a quiet residential streetscape.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen's guiding principle is simplicity in service of the ingredient: boiling, grilling, and steaming are used to bring out natural flavors, with ancient salt and aged soy sauce as seasonings, and bincho charcoal for all grilled items. The motto is to let carefully sourced ingredients speak for themselves.
chef_bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled seasonal ingredients: single-night-dried fish sourced from around Japan, three-variety Japanese heritage chicken (Akita Hinai, Nagoya Cochin, Satsuma Jidori), Kumamoto horse-meat sashimi, and wagyu loin grilled tableside over bincho charcoal.

anecdotes.text: Head chef Fukute Haruhiko works at the open counter, grilling directly-sourced overnight-dried fish over live bincho charcoal within view of guests — a deliberate choice that makes the craft of the kitchen part of the dining experience.

The dining room is designed so that every seat — counter, private room, and upstairs — faces the Japanese garden. A glass floor section in one room has garden stones embedded beneath it, blurring the line between interior and exterior.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: lade.jp（食べログ記録）が築70年の古民家改装・白壁地区・尼ヶ坂駅至近・2005年11月開業/2025年10月改装再開・料理長 福手春彦・備長炭のカウンター焼き・一夜干し・比内地鶏/名古屋コーチン/薩摩地鶏・熊本の馬刺し・和牛・全席が庭に面する設計・庭石を敷いたガラス床を確認。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tempura appears in multiple set courses and à la carte dishes. No information on a dedicated fryer or separation from other fried items.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Aged soy sauce (熟成醤油) is a stated house seasoning used across grilled, simmered, and rice dishes. Miso-based preparations also appear throughout the menu. Both contain wheat.
Charcoal-grilled rice balls with shoyu glaze and soy-glazed bamboo shoots are among listed dishes — standard wheat-containing soy sauce is used.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Fish-based dashi is a foundational ingredient in Japanese kappo cooking and is almost certainly present in soups and simmered dishes even where not explicitly listed.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Multiple sources report that the restaurant accommodates allergy requests when notified in advance. No formal allergy protocol or written allergen menu is documented.

positives.text: The kitchen uses high-quality ancient salt (古代塩) as a primary seasoning alongside soy sauce, meaning some simply-salted grilled items may be free of soy sauce and wheat — worth asking about specifically.

cultural_comfort.note: Traditional kappo setting with tatami-style private rooms; staff are hospitable but the environment is formal Japanese dining — no English menus confirmed, and advance reservations are required.

places.name: 花いち (Hanaichi)
places.gf_detail: Wheat is present throughout: tempura batter (signature tenmusu), soy sauce (used in seasoning), kitsune udon (wheat noodles), and dashimaki tamago. No gluten-free menu or substitution system is documented anywhere. Given the reservation-only, fully-booked format, diners with celiac should contact the restaurant far in advance — though no evidence exists that GF accommodations have been made.
places.vegan_detail: Traditional kappo cuisine built on fish-based dashi (the chef intentionally avoids kombu dashi, preferring fish stock) and seasonal seafood as central ingredients. No vegan alternatives are offered. Signature dishes include shrimp tempura tenmusu and dashimaki tamago.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu–Sun 17:00–22:00 (L.O. 21:30), closed Wed and holidays
places.neighborhood: Nishi-ku, Kodama (~8–10 min walk from Jōshin Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Born in Hekinan, Aichi, Ginzo Hanaichi left home at 16 for Osaka to apprentice in kappo and sushi restaurants. At 29 he returned to Nagoya and in 1983 opened 花いち in a quiet residential street in Nishi Ward — a converted traditional house with no exterior signage. Over 40-plus yea

chef_bio.origin: 愛知県碧南市 (Hekinan, Aichi Prefecture)
chef_bio.background: Born in Hekinan, Aichi, Ginzo Hanaichi left home at 16 for Osaka to apprentice in kappo and sushi restaurants. He later returned to Nagoya and in 1983 opened 花いち on a quiet residential street in Nishi Ward — a converted traditional house with no exterior signage. Over 40-plus years he has kept the kappo format unchanged, cooking entirely from scratch before guests each evening and sourcing daily at Yanagibashi Central Market.
chef_bio.philosophy: The chef coined the phrase 不触不味 — roughly, 'do not touch what you would ruin by touching' — meaning the less you manipulate an ingredient the truer its flavor. He avoids kombu dashi entirely, believing it overwhelms seasonal produce. Nothing is made in advance; every dish is cooked to order.
chef_bio.specialty: Tenmusu (shrimp tempura rice ball) and freshly fried hanpen (fish cake with burdock) — dishes that distill the chef's obsession with serving food at the precise moment it is done.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant has no sign on the door. A single wooden board bearing the brushed characters 不触不味 hangs under the eaves — that is the only outward mark of one of Nagoya's most-awarded restaurants.

Regulars joke that the chef's voice is heard so rarely it is practically a menu item: '会話なら料理として' — 'if you want conversation, order it as a dish.' His wife Toshie (年恵) manages all hospitality while he focuses entirely on the counter.

The daily handwritten menu is numbered by the restaurant's operating days — one visitor noted they were on day 9,603. After 40 years, the counter seats and the menu board look much as they did when 花いち opened in 1983.
As of the time of writing, new reservations are not being accepted; the restaurant is reportedly booked more than two years ahead.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: watobi.jpが花市銀蔵氏（碧南出身・16歳で大阪で割烹/寿司修業・1983年西区で開業）・看板を出さず軒下の『不触不味』の板・昆布出汁を使わない方針・妻年恵(トシエ)の接客・柳橋中央市場での毎日の仕入れ・注文毎の調理を確認。menage.jpがミシュラン一つ星を示唆、nagoya-meshiが手書きの営業日数メニュー、hobogifuが約2年待ちを伝える。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tempura (shrimp, squid, dried potato) is fried on the counter in front of guests — a shared cooking environment where wheat-containing tempura batter is in active use throughout service.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard wheat-containing soy sauce is used in seasoning. No tamari or GF soy sauce substitution is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Fish-based dashi underpins most preparations. The chef explicitly avoids kombu dashi in favour of fish stock, making an all-plant meal structurally incompatible with the kitchen's approach.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen menu, substitution policy, or allergy-handling protocol is documented in any reviewed source. Given the reservation-only format booked years in advance, allergen requests would need to be raised at the time of booking.

positives.text: Every dish is cooked entirely from scratch to order — no advance preparation or pre-made sauces — which means the chef has full visibility of what goes into each dish at each moment of service.

cultural_comfort.note: No English signage or menu; the chef is famously quiet and interaction flows primarily through his wife who manages hospitality — comfortable for Japanese-speaking diners, challenging for non-Japanese speakers.

places.name: 焼鳥 hanauta (Yakitori Hanauta)
places.gf_detail: Standard yakitori tare is made with wheat-containing soy sauce. Salt-grilled (shio) skewers are the lower-risk option but the same grill handles tare-glazed pieces, creating cross-contamination risk. No dedicated gluten-free facility or certified GF menu was found. Celiac diners should call ahead; the intimate 8-seat counter format may allow chef accommodation, but nothing is confirmed.
places.vegan_detail: Entirely chicken-focused omakase. Every course centers on binchotan-grilled heritage chicken skewers. No plant-based alternative course is offered.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Sun 17:00–23:00, closed Mon
places.neighborhood: Kokusai Center / Nagono (Endōji shopping street)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened in June 2023 on the second floor of a building in the historic Entoji shopping street (円頓寺商店街) district of Nagoya's Nishi Ward. The restaurant occupies a small but stylish space, part of the Kinsyachi Shoutengai merchant collective.

chef_bio.chef_name: Masaya (given name only, per Instagram @hanauta_masaya; surname not publicly disclosed)
chef_bio.background: Opened June 16, 2023 on the second floor of a building in the historic Entoji shopping street (円頓寺商店街) area of Nagoya's Nishi Ward (Nakono 2-chome). The restaurant is a small but stylish 8-seat counter space, part of the Kinsyachi Shoutengai (金シャチ商店街) merchant collective.
chef_bio.philosophy: Precision over quantity: an intimate course built around the quality of the bird, the exactness of the heat, and the seasoning balance. Natural wines and local sake are chosen to complement, not overshadow, the grill.
chef_bio.specialty: Binchotan charcoal-grilled heritage chicken skewers — the kitchen centers on female Omi jidori (淡海地鶏メス), and also uses breeds such as Amakusa Daio (天草大王) and Mikawa aka-dori (三河赤鶏), sourced for flavor and breed integrity.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers single out the chef's focus on female birds — a deliberate sourcing choice one reviewer noted is unusual for yakitori ('焼鳥で雌にこだわったお店は初めてでした'). Another wrote the heat and salt balance over binchotan was exactly right across every skewer.
The shop fills largely with repeat customers, a sign of how quickly a loyal clientele formed around the 8-seat counter since its June 2023 opening.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 金シャチ商店街公式・食べログによれば、2023年6月16日に名古屋市西区那古野(円頓寺商店街)の2階にオープンしたカウンター8席の小さな焼鳥屋。備長炭焼きで、雌の淡海地鶏をはじめ天草大王・三河赤鶏など銘柄鶏にこだわる。店主の名は公表されず、Instagram(@hanauta_masaya)から下の名『Masaya』のみ確認できる。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tare-glazed and shio (salt) skewers share the same binchotan grill. Cross-contamination from wheat-based tare drips or brush contact is likely for celiac-risk diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Yakitori tare is traditionally made with soy sauce (containing wheat). No gluten-free soy sauce alternative was mentioned anywhere in available sources.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire menu is built around grilled chicken; there is no plant-based course and vegan cross-contact is near-certain.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy-handling policy or staff training information is publicly documented. The 8-seat counter format does allow direct communication with the chef, which may make individual requests possible — call ahead.

positives.text: Shio (salt-only) skewers are available and may avoid direct tare/wheat contact for lower-sensitivity guests, though shared grill risk remains.

cultural_comfort.note: An intimate 8-seat counter where conversation with the chef is part of the experience; basic Japanese will help, though the warm, approachable atmosphere is noted by regulars.

places.name: スパイシーカレー クスクス 白壁店 (Spicy Curry Kusukusu Shirakabe)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free designation anywhere. Naan is wheat-based and the default bread; basmati rice is available as an alternative and is naturally GF. Indian/Nepalese-style curry sauces are not typically thickened with wheat flour, but cross-contamination from naan baking in a shared kitchen is a realistic risk. No dedicated fryer or allergen protocol is documented. Celiac diners should call ahead and confirm rice-only ordering, shared cooking surfaces, and any wheat-containing marinades or sauces.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan designation on the menu. Curries are likely made with ghee or cream in some preparations (Malai Tikka, butter-based sauces). A rice-based set without dairy toppings may be achievable but would require direct confirmation with staff. The spice-fried chicken and kebabs contain meat; no plant-based curry option is advertised.
places.neighborhood: 東大手・白壁 (Higashi Ward)
places.notes: The Shirakabe branch is the second location of a well-regarded Nagoya curry operation that traces its roots to a shop called Kushukusu (クシュクシュ) in Midori Ward's Takino neighborhood. After closing and relocating, the concept was reborn as Kusukusu at a Nonami (Tenpaku Ward) addres

chef_bio.background: The Shirakabe branch is the second location of a well-regarded Nagoya curry operation that traces its roots to a shop called Kushukusu (クシュクシュ) in Midori Ward's Takinomizu (滝ノ水) neighborhood. After closing and relocating, the concept was reborn as Kusukusu at a Nonami (Tenpaku Ward) address, building a loyal following before opening this Shirakabe outpost in January 2024 — a 1-minute walk from Meitetsu Seto Line Higashi-Ote Station and within walking distance of Nagoya Castle.
chef_bio.philosophy: Authentic India-Nepal spice cooking aimed at Japanese palates, balancing acidity, spice complexity, and sweetness rather than raw heat alone, with a 1–5 spice-level choice. Monthly limited specials keep the menu rotating.
chef_bio.specialty: Marwani/Malwani curry (マルワニカレー) — a rare Western Indian preparation, described by reviewers as a thin, flowing, aromatic tomato-and-onion-based sauce that builds heat gradually.

anecdotes.text: A Nagoya food blog noted the signage reads 'Indo-Nepal Spicy Curry' and praised the marwani curry as one of the few places in Japan serving this regional Western Indian dish — the curry described as 'a sea of sauce with rice as an island.'

The nagoya-meshi.com review invokes the idiom '八面六臂' (eight-faced, six-armed — remarkably versatile) in describing how the kitchen handles everything from spice-fried chicken to creamy malai tikka to this unusual marwani preparation.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 地元ブログ(tansin-nagoya)とnagoya-meshi.comによると、緑区滝ノ水の『クシュクシュ』を源流とし、天白区野並へ移転して『クスクス』に。2024年1月に白壁店(名鉄瀬戸線 東大手駅至近)を開店。ネパール人経営の印度・ネパール料理で、西インドのマルワニカレー(サラサラで玉ねぎ・トマトベース)が名物。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Naan (wheat) is baked on-premises and is the default bread; the kitchen handles wheat continuously. No dedicated GF prep area or protocol is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Indian-Nepalese curry cuisine does not typically use soy sauce; no soy sauce is mentioned in any source reviewed. However, no allergen statement has been published by the restaurant, so this cannot be guaranteed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Ghee, yogurt, and cream are standard in multiple menu items (Malai Tikka, butter-based preparations). A shared kitchen serving meat and dairy means cross-contact for vegan diners is probable without explicit accommodation.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy-handling policy or allergen menu has been published. Phone reservations are noted as unavailable (online booking only), making pre-visit allergy communication more difficult — diners should plan to ask at the counter on arrival.

positives.text: Basmati rice is available as a wheat-free alternative to naan and can be ordered as part of the set meal, offering a naturally gluten-free carb base.

cultural_comfort.note: Nepalese-run neighborhood curry shop near Nagoya Castle; menu is accessible and set meals are easy to order, though staff likely communicate primarily in Japanese.

places.name: 名古屋城きしめん亭 (Nagoyajo Kishimen-tei)
places.gf_detail: Kishimen is a flat wheat noodle — gluten is the core ingredient and cannot be substituted. The soy sauce used in the dashi broth also contains wheat. There is no gluten-free alternative on the menu. Deep-fried items (tempura, kushi-age) share the kitchen environment. This restaurant is not suitable for anyone with celiac disease or serious gluten intolerance.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan options. All kishimen dishes are made with a rich bonito (katsuo) and fish-based dashi broth. Standard toppings include bonito flakes, fried tofu (aburaage, which may share fryer oil), and wakame. No plant-based broth alternative is available. Set meals include inari sushi (which contains dashi-seasoned rice) and miso-breaded cutlets with pork.
places.hours_raw: 10:00–16:00 (last order around 16:15–16:30 per some sources); closed Dec 29–31
places.neighborhood: Naka-ku, Honmaru (inside Nagoya Castle) / Nagoyajō Stn
places.notes: Nagoyajo Kishimen-tei has operated inside Nagoya Castle since 1972 (Showa 47), run by the Aichi Prefectural Noodle Association (愛知県めんるい組合), the trade body representing the region's udon and soba makers. The association holds the concession to serve noodles on the castle grounds,

roles: Aichi Prefectural Noodle Association (愛知県めんるい組合) — operator since 1972
chef_bio.background: Nagoyajo Kishimen-tei has operated inside Nagoya Castle since 1972 (Showa 47), run by the Aichi Prefectural Noodle Association (愛知県めんるい組合), the trade body for the region's udon and soba makers. It is cited as the association's only official restaurant within the castle grounds.
chef_bio.philosophy: Simple, honest noodle-stand cooking rooted in Nagoya's kishimen tradition, served to castle visitors at accessible prices in an open, self-service format.
chef_bio.specialty: Nagoya-style kishimen — wide, flat wheat noodles in a deep bonito-and-soy dashi, topped with wakame, sweet aburaage fried tofu, and bonito flakes. The classic warm bowl (名物きしめん) starts at ¥550; a cold version (きしころ, ¥600) is also offered.

anecdotes.text: A local food blog passes along a rumor that the Nagoya mayor secretly comes to eat kishimen here ('市長もこっそり食べに来るという噂も') — treated as an open secret among regulars.
The stand uses a modern touch-screen ticket vending machine with menus in English, Chinese, and Korean — a notably high-tech touch for a no-frills castle noodle stand.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: xtone・busho-heart・nagoya-info・公式観光サイトによると、昭和47年(1972年)から名古屋城内で営業する、愛知県めんるい組合の城内唯一の公式店。名物きしめん550円、冷やしのきしころ600円、わかめ・油揚げ・かつお節をのせる。多言語対応のタッチパネル券売機、市長がこっそり来るという噂も。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Kishimen noodles are made from wheat flour — gluten is the primary ingredient. No GF alternative exists. The kitchen also prepares tempura and fried items in the same space.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dashi broth is seasoned with standard soy sauce, which contains wheat. No tamari or gluten-free soy sauce option is mentioned anywhere.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bonito flakes (katsuobushi) are a direct topping on kishimen and are also used in the broth base. There is no plant-based broth option. All menu items contain animal-derived ingredients.

cultural_comfort.note: The ticket vending machine supports English, Chinese, and Korean, making ordering straightforward for non-Japanese speakers, though staff interaction beyond order pickup is minimal.

places.name: なごや えんきょ (Nagoya Enkyo)
places.gf_detail: Traditional kaiseki format with high likelihood of wheat in standard Japanese soy sauce (shoyu), and fish-based dashi almost certain throughout courses. Red miso soup is listed as part of every course. No gluten-free menu or dedicated accommodations are advertised. The website says to notify of allergies in advance, but makes no GF-safe promises. Cross-contamination risk in a small kitchen producing omakase courses is real. Celiacs should call ahead and treat this as "ask" at best.
places.vegan_detail: Full-course kaiseki centred on premium seafood (sashimi, fish dishes) and A5 wagyu beef, with courses including miso soup and dashi-based dishes. No vegan course or plant-based alternative is listed. Hidden animal-derived dashi (katsuo/bonito or niboshi) is standard in this style of cooking. Vegans should inquire well in advance — significant menu restructuring would be required and is not guaranteed.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sat lunch 11:30–14:00 (L.O. 13:30), dinner 17:30–23:00 (L.O. 21:30). Closed Sun & holidays (negotiable with advance reservation).
places.neighborhood: Marunouchi / Hisaya-ōdōri
places.notes: Born 1969 in Aichi Prefecture and raised in a washoku chef's household, Nagashima has spent over 40 years in Japanese cuisine. He has been visiting Nagoya's Yanagibashi Market daily for more than 30 years, building relationships with vendors and selecting fish, produce, and premi

chef_bio.origin: The restaurant originally operated under the name 円居 (Enkyo) for around 20 years before relocating and reopening under the current name なごや えんきょ in the Marunouchi ia Building.
chef_bio.background: Born 1969 in Aichi Prefecture and raised in a washoku chef's household, Nagashima has spent over 40 years in Japanese cuisine. He has visited Nagoya's Yanagibashi Market daily for more than 30 years, building relationships with vendors and selecting fish, produce, and premium beef by eye each morning. He has also made personal visits to California wine producers — particularly Beringer — to help curate the restaurant's wine list.
chef_bio.philosophy: Drawing out the original deliciousness of seasonal ingredients through careful sourcing and creative refinement rather than heavy elaboration, pairing Japanese culinary tradition with an adventurous approach to wine that is unusual for the genre.
chef_bio.specialty: Omakase courses built around whatever Nagashima considers finest at market that day — sashimi, seasonal fish, A5 wagyu, and rice dishes are recurring highlights, with black truffle rice a noted signature.

anecdotes.text: Nagashima has visited Yanagibashi Market every day for over 30 years, selecting ingredients personally — a practice he describes as the foundation of the restaurant's quality.

To build the wine list, Nagashima has travelled to California wineries, including Beringer, to taste and select bottles directly — a rare cross-cultural commitment for a traditional kaiseki-style restaurant.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: ヒトサラによると、店主・長島正典は1969年愛知県生まれ、和食料理人の家に育ち、料理歴40年以上。柳橋市場に30年以上毎日通い自らの目利きで仕入れる。カリフォルニア(ベリンジャー等)のワイナリーを自ら訪ねてワインを選ぶ。旧店名『円居』を約20年営んだ後、丸の内iaビルに移転・改称。A5和牛や黒トリュフご飯が名物。

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated gluten-free kitchen or cross-contamination controls are described. Omakase production in a compact kitchen handling soy sauce, miso, and breaded or battered items creates real shared-surface risk.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce (containing wheat) is almost certainly used throughout. No tamari or wheat-free soy sauce alternative is mentioned anywhere on the official site or third-party listings.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All courses include fish-based dashi (red dashi miso soup confirmed), sashimi, and meat. Animal products are integral to every course component; no plant-only preparation line exists.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The menu page asks guests to notify the restaurant of allergies in advance, but no specific allergy protocol, substitution policy, or staff training is described.

positives.text: Advance notice is explicitly requested for allergies, and the intimate counter format means the single chef oversees all preparation — reducing the risk of miscommunication between kitchen staff compared to large restaurants.

cultural_comfort.note: Small counter-only kaiseki restaurant where Japanese is the working language; no English materials or bilingual staff noted, and the intimate 14-seat format assumes familiarity with kaiseki etiquette.

places.name: ほしのこ食堂 (Hoshinoko Shokudo)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu or allergen information is published. Standard Japanese cooking relies heavily on wheat-containing soy sauce (shoyu), and baked goods on the menu (butter cookies, pound cake, bolo) contain wheat flour. One source notes the owner uses naturally fermented soy sauce, but whether tamari or GF alternatives are available is unknown. The kitchen is a tiny single-operator counter with no dedicated fryer information available. Celiacs should contact the restaurant in advance via Instagram; do not assume safety.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is traditional Japanese canteen cooking — seasonal bento boxes, simmered side dishes, and baked goods. Standard Japanese ingredients such as dashi (likely fish-based), katsuo (bonito), and egg are almost certainly present in many items. No vegan or plant-based menu has been advertised. Ask directly on the day; some vegetable side dishes may incidentally be vegan, but this cannot be assumed.
places.hours_raw: Operating days announced via Instagram (@hoshinoko_shokudou)
places.neighborhood: 西区 上名古屋 (Kaminagoya), Nishi Ward
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Miyata Seiko, born 1979, trained at a culinary academy straight out of high school before spending years working in welfare and care facilities. The hands-on experience feeding vulnerable people in medical and social-care settings convinced her to pursue the highest nutritional c

chef_bio.background: Miyata Seiko, from Iwakura City in Aichi, trained at a culinary academy straight out of high school and then worked as a licensed cook (調理師) in welfare/care facilities. That hands-on experience feeding people in social-care settings led her to pursue the registered-dietitian credential: she enrolled in a nutritionist academy, passed the national registered dietitian (管理栄養士) exam, and went on to work as a dietitian in a hospital and a kindergarten. She holds both a cooking chef license and the registered dietitian qualification.
chef_bio.philosophy: Everyday food people actually want to eat every day — unpretentious, body-conscious, and ingredient-honest. The menu rotates with the season and keeps flavour and restraint in balance.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal Japanese bento boxes and simple baked sweets — including cookies and pound/chocolate cakes — made with consciously sourced ingredients such as naturally fermented (天然醸造) soy sauce.

anecdotes.text: At Nagoya's monthly Higashi Betsuin morning market (the 28th of each month), Hoshinoko Shokudo regularly drew queues of 20 or more ('20名以上') before the stall opened — regulars learned to arrive early or miss out.
After years of market and event pop-ups based in Nagoya, the shop opened a fixed 4-seat counter location in Nishi Ward (Kaminagoya) on January 12, 2026.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Y.YACHT STOREのプロフィールによると、宮田せい子は愛知県岩倉市出身。高校卒業後に調理専門学校へ進み調理師として福祉施設で勤務、その後栄養士専門学校を経て管理栄養士国家試験に合格し病院・幼稚園でも勤務。2017年に『ほしのこ食堂』を開始、&(COOK)へ記事執筆、料理教室も。internal-reformは東別院朝市(毎月28日)で20名以上の行列、食べログは2026年1月12日に西区上名古屋にカウンター4席の店舗を開店と記載。生年は明記なし。

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen is a tiny 4-seat solo-run counter with no information published about cross-contamination controls; wheat flour is used in baked goods on the menu, making cross-contact plausible.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese cooking is the base; soy sauce (wheat-containing shoyu) is almost certainly used in simmered dishes and bento items. One source notes naturally fermented soy sauce is used, but no tamari or wheat-free alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Fish-based dashi and egg are standard in Japanese canteen cooking of this style; no plant-based or vegan-separated preparation has been mentioned.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy handling protocol is published. The owner is a registered dietitian, so ingredient awareness is high, but no formal allergen accommodation system has been publicly described.

positives.text: Owner holds registered dietitian and culinary chef qualifications, suggesting strong ingredient awareness and nutritional care in menu planning.
A focus on conscientious sourcing and body-conscious ingredients has been a consistent part of the brand's identity since its market-stall days.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny solo-run counter with no English signage or menu; photography of food, interior, and chef is prohibited, suggesting an intimate local atmosphere where basic Japanese or pointing at the day's dishes is the expected way to order.

places.name: じらい亭 (Jirai Tei)
places.gf_detail: The entire menu is built around wheat: ramen noodles (sourced from Fukuoka), wheat-based soy sauce in the house tare and custom oil blend, and the Taiwan mazesoba sauce. The review also notes tempura and Chinese dishes on the menu. No gluten-free options are available, no dedicated fryer information found, and there is no indication of any allergy accommodation. Cross-contamination risk is high in this small counter kitchen.
places.vegan_detail: This is a traditional izakaya with a pork-forward menu — signature dishes are tonkotsu (pork bone) ramen and Taiwan mazesoba with minced pork. No vegan options are available or implied. Animal products are central to every signature dish.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Sat 17:30–22:00 (L.O. 21:00), closed Mon and Sun
places.neighborhood: Sengen Cho (浅間町), Nishi Ward
places.cuisine: Izakaya / Taiwanese mixed noodles / Hakata t
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Chef Kuwano Toshiaki grew up near Kurume in Fukuoka, the birthplace of Hakata tonkotsu ramen, and brought that pedigree to this compact neighborhood izakaya in Nagoya's Nishi Ward. The restaurant's thin Kyushu-sourced noodles are imported directly from the chef's home region.

chef_bio.background: Chef Kuwano Toshiaki grew up near Kurume in Fukuoka — Hakata tonkotsu country — and brought that pedigree to this compact neighborhood izakaya in Nagoya's Nishi Ward. The restaurant's thin noodles are imported directly from his home region in Kyushu.
chef_bio.philosophy: Authentic, generous, and deeply flavored — the chef has said he will keep sourcing noodles from Kyushu for as long as he can maintain his standards.
chef_bio.specialty: 台湾まぜそば (Taiwan mazesoba) — thin Kyushu noodles tossed with a secret soy-based tare, a proprietary three-oil blend, minced pork, garlic, chili, bean sprouts, and Chinese chives, wok-fired at high heat for a crispy finish. Priced at ¥800.

anecdotes.text: The Taiwan mazesoba was never planned. It grew out of customers asking to add noodles to the popular Taiwan bean-sprout stir-fry side dish, and became the signature that now books the restaurant a month or more in advance.
Reservation waits run one to one-and-a-half months, and the 21-seat counter draws ramen enthusiasts who travel considerable distances — including from Gifu — for a seat.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: HICBC(CHANT特集)によると、店主・桑野俊昭は福岡・久留米出身で、九州(福岡)の極細ラーメン麺を直接仕入れる。名物の台湾まぜそばは、人気の台湾もやし炒めに麺を入れてほしいという常連の要望から偶然生まれたもの。秘伝の醤油だれと三種のオイルを使い高温で炒める。約21席、予約は1〜1.5ヶ月待ちで遠方からも客が訪れる。

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated gluten-free prep area or fryer has been identified. The kitchen operates as a small open counter and cooks ramen noodles, wheat-based sauces, and other wheat-containing dishes simultaneously. Cross-contamination risk should be assumed high.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The Taiwan mazesoba uses a house-made soy-based tare as its primary seasoning. Standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. No tamari or gluten-free soy sauce alternative is mentioned anywhere.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Pork (minced, in broth) and other animal products are present in every dish. The tonkotsu ramen broth is pork-bone based. No vegan-safe preparation is possible at this restaurant.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy handling protocol, allergen menu, or dietary accommodation was found in any source. The restaurant has no English materials. Diners with serious food allergies should contact the restaurant directly by phone (052-524-0498) in Japanese before visiting.

cultural_comfort.note: Counter-only izakaya with no English signage or menu; reservations are typically made by phone in Japanese, and the small intimate counter means direct interaction with the chef in Japanese is expected.

places.name: ガーブ カステッロ (GARB CASTELLO)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu or allergen chart is published. The kitchen centres on wood-fired Neapolitan pizza (wheat dough) and Italian pasta — wheat is present throughout the kitchen. No dedicated GF fryer or GF pizza base is mentioned anywhere. Soy sauce use is not confirmed but typical Italian preparations may employ wheat-containing condiments. Celiacs should contact the restaurant before visiting; safe options cannot be assumed.
places.vegan_detail: No dedicated vegan menu or labelling found. Italian menu includes pasta, pizza, meat, and fish; dairy and eggs appear throughout. Some vegetable-focused antipasti and pizza variants may exist but no explicit vegan options are published. Guests would need to ask staff about preparation specifics.
places.neighborhood: Kita-ku / Meijō Park
places.notes: GARB CASTELLO opened on April 27, 2017, as the anchor dining venue of tonarino, a commercial facility built within Nagoya's Meijo Park directly adjacent to Nagoya Castle. It is operated by Balnibarbi Corporation, a Osaka- and Tokyo-based restaurant group founded in 1991 and liste

chef_bio.origin: 名古屋市北区名城公園 (Meijo Park, Kita Ward, Nagoya)
chef_bio.background: GARB CASTELLO opened on April 27, 2017 as the anchor dining venue of tonarino, a commercial facility built within Nagoya's Meijo Park directly adjacent to Nagoya Castle. It is operated by Balnibarbi Corporation, an Osaka-headquartered restaurant group founded in 1991 and listed on the Tokyo Stock Exchange Growth market, known for large-scale lifestyle restaurants in park and waterfront settings.
chef_bio.philosophy: The guiding idea is '寛ぎの時間は、緑とともに' — relaxed, nature-immersed dining where the park landscape and views of Nagoya Castle become part of the experience. The space suits families, couples, and large groups, with a pet-friendly terrace, year-round outdoor BBQ, and capacity of around 230 across two floors and terraces.
chef_bio.specialty: 薪釜ピッツァ (wood-fired Neapolitan pizza) baked in a live-fire oven, alongside a broad Italian menu of pasta, meat, and fish dishes.

anecdotes.text: The tonarino complex was developed under the concept '城の隣、あなたの隣' (next to the castle, next to you), positioning the venue as an everyday gathering place for park visitors and local residents.
The restaurant offers year-round terrace BBQ across three tiered plans (¥3,000–¥5,000 per person), one of the few park-side BBQ options in central Nagoya.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: バルニバービ公式・PR TIMESによると、GARB CASTELLOは2017年4月27日、名城公園内の『tonarino』にオープン。運営は1991年設立・東証グロース上場の(株)バルニバービ(大阪本社)。薪釜のナポリピッツァが看板で、約230席、テラスでは通年BBQ(3,000〜5,000円の3プラン)。『城の隣、あなたの隣』がtonarinoのコンセプト。

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen is built around wheat-based pizza dough and pasta; cross-contamination with gluten must be assumed throughout. No allergen separation or dedicated preparation area is mentioned in any source.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No information on soy sauce use is published. Italian kitchens may use wheat-containing condiments and sauces; cannot be confirmed or ruled out without contacting the restaurant.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dairy (cheese, cream) and eggs appear throughout the pizza and pasta menu. No vegan-separated preparation is indicated.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen menu, allergy-handling protocol, or staff training information is published on the official site or any third-party source reviewed.

cultural_comfort.note: Large park-facing restaurant run by a listed Tokyo restaurant group; menus in Japanese but staff at a popular tourist-adjacent venue are accustomed to non-Japanese guests.

places.name: めん処まるいち (Mendokoro Maruichi)
places.gf_detail: The restaurant serves both soba and udon, with noodles described as pure handmade (純手打ち). Japanese soba at most small shops is a blended ratio — typically 80/20 or 70/30 buckwheat-to-wheat — rather than 100% buckwheat (juwari). No 100% buckwheat claim is made anywhere in available sources. Udon is entirely wheat-based. The kitchen also prepares tempura and chicken katsu, creating significant cross-contamination risk. There is no dedicated gluten-free preparation noted. Celiacs should avoid; those with mild sensitivity should call ahead.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu exists. The kitchen uses bonito-based dashi in noodle broths, and the menu includes chicken katsu, dashi-maki tamago, and tempura dishes. Plant-based diners would need to ask carefully about every item; a safe order is unlikely without advance communication.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sat 11:30–14:00 (L.O. 14:00) / 17:30–22:00 (L.O. 21:30); Sunday closed (open on public holidays)
places.neighborhood: Jōshin / Hananoki, Nishi Ward
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A compact, traditional noodle shop a short walk from Joshin Station in Nagoya's Nishi Ward, run by a young proprietor. The shop occupies the ground floor of a residential building and seats just 21 people across a counter and a tatami room with sunken seating, giving it the feel

chef_bio.background: A compact, traditional noodle shop about 10 minutes' walk from Joshin Station in Nagoya's Nishi Ward. It occupies the ground floor of a residential building and seats 21 across a 5-seat counter and a 16-seat tatami room with sunken (kotatsu-style) seating, giving it the feel of a neighbourhood canteen rather than a formal restaurant.
chef_bio.philosophy: Volume-forward, everyday cooking using fresh ingredients, with menu variety that shifts based on what is good that day.
chef_bio.specialty: Handmade udon and soba (純手打ちうどん・そば) with a rotating daily-board menu. Lunch draws locals for sets around ¥780; evenings shift toward small plates and drinks. The house-made soy-sauce chicken katsu bowl (自家製醤油のチキンカツ丼) is a noted signature, and reviewers also praise the oroshi kishimen.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers describe a homey atmosphere — 'like returning home' (自宅に帰ったようなアットホームな雰囲気) — and the owner is consistently praised for a warm, welcoming manner.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 食べログによると、名古屋市西区・城信駅から徒歩約10分の手打ちうどん・そばの店。カウンター5席+掘りごたつの座敷16席で計21席。ランチのセットは780円前後。肉厚のチキンカツに自家製しょうゆが合うチキンカツ丼やおろしきしめんが好評で、自宅に帰ったようなアットホームな雰囲気と店主の温かい接客が評価されている。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tempura and breaded katsu are prepared in the same kitchen. No separate fryer or preparation area for gluten-free orders is mentioned anywhere in available sources.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes use a house-blend soy sauce (自家製醤油) for items like the chicken katsu bowl; conventional Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. No tamari or gluten-free soy sauce alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths are described as using bonito-based dashi (かつお出汁). Tempura and dashi-maki tamago (egg) are prominent menu items, making a plant-free meal unlikely without special arrangement.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen menu, allergy protocol, or staff training for dietary restrictions is noted in any available source. The restaurant operates without credit card machines and with a chalkboard-only menu, suggesting a basic neighbourhood setup without formal allergen systems.

positives.text: The young owner is consistently described as approachable and communicative — a direct phone call (052-524-6555) ahead of a visit is the most practical path for anyone with dietary needs.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional neighborhood noodle shop with a daily-board menu written in Japanese; no English signage or menus expected, but the young owner is noted as friendly and approachable.

places.name: 鮨うおのたな (Sushi Uonotana)
places.gf_detail: Standard wheat-based soy sauce (shoyu) is used throughout, as is typical in Edomae sushi. The TableCheck reservation page explicitly states the restaurant cannot accommodate allergies arising from shared frying oil or shared cooking utensils. No dedicated GF menu or GF soy sauce alternative is advertised. Celiac diners should contact the restaurant directly before booking.
places.vegan_detail: Standard Edomae sushi — fish is the centerpiece of every course. No vegan course or substitutions are offered. Dashi is fish-based; tamago (egg) is standard. Not suitable for vegans.
places.neighborhood: Naka-ku, Marunouchi
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Sushi Uonotana opened on September 16, 2025, as one of two new dining concepts hidden inside 河文 (Kawabun), a historic kaiseki ryotei that has anchored Nagoya's Marunouchi district for over 400 years. Guests once served the Owari Tokugawa clan passed through Kawabun's grand entran

roles: female head sushi chef (name not disclosed publicly)
operated by Plan・Do・See hospitality group
chef_bio.origin: 名古屋市中区丸の内 / within 河文 (Kawabun ryotei)
chef_bio.background: Sushi Uonotana opened on September 16, 2025 as a new dining concept hidden inside 河文 (Kawabun), a historic kaiseki ryotei that has anchored Nagoya's Marunouchi district for some 400 years, with ties to the Owari Tokugawa clan. Diners slip in through the back service entrance — the 勝手口 — to reach an 11-seat counter separate in spirit from the formal ryotei. It is operated by the hospitality group Plan・Do・See and occupies renovated back-of-house space in Kawabun's historic building. The head chef is a woman — still uncommon in Edomae sushi — whose name is not publicly disclosed.
chef_bio.philosophy: Approachable authentic Edomae sushi — the stated concept is '本格江戸前鮨を気軽に楽しむ' (enjoy authentic Edomae sushi casually): traditional Tokyo-style technique on an accessible counter inside a historic ryotei, with care given equally to craftsmanship and hospitality.
chef_bio.specialty: Edomae omakase — lunch ¥3,800 and dinner ¥7,800, featuring seasonal nigiri including kombu-jime sea bream, marinated (zuke) tuna, and salmon roe (ikura), finished with red miso soup (aka-dashi) and tamago.

anecdotes.text: The entrance has no prominent signage. Diners walk through the service alley (勝手口) of a roughly 400-year-old ryotei and find an 11-seat counter that feels like a private discovery.

A reviewer who tried the ¥3,800 lunch in late 2025 called it 'top-class value' (コスパ最強クラス) and noted the red-vinegar shari was 'easy for Nagoya people to eat' (名古屋人でも食べやすいタイプ) — a nod to the difference between Edo-style shari and local palates.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: PR TIMES・CentralWalker・食べログによると、2025年9月16日、約400年続く老舗料亭『河文』の中に開業。勝手口から入るカウンター11席で、運営はPlan・Do・See。握り手は業界では珍しい女性(氏名非公表)。『本格江戸前鮨を気軽に楽しむ』が概念で、昼3,800円・夜7,800円。昆布〆の鯛、鮪のヅケ、いくら、赤だし、玉子など。レビューでは『コスパ最強クラス』『名古屋人でも食べやすい』赤酢のシャリと評される。

gf_cross_contamination.text: The TableCheck reservation page explicitly states: 'We cannot accommodate requests if allergic reactions occur due to the use of the same frying oil or cooking utensils, or the manufacturing process.' Shared equipment is confirmed — cross-contamination risk is real.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard wheat-containing soy sauce is used throughout the omakase — marinades (漬け maguro), brushed nigiri finishing, and dipping are all standard Edomae practice. No tamari or GF soy sauce alternative is mentioned anywhere.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Fish and seafood are the core of every course. Dashi used in the kitchen (including in the adjacent 勝手口 河内屋) is fish-based. No vegan-safe preparation track exists.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Allergy accommodations with ingredient modifications are available per the TableCheck page, but with explicit caveats: shared frying oil and shared cooking utensils are not manageable. Guests with severe allergies should call 052-211-8322 (10:30–19:00) before booking.

positives.text: Counter format means diners can watch preparation directly and speak with the chef — the best environment to communicate dietary needs in real time.

cultural_comfort.note: Counter-only omakase in a traditional hidden setting; reservations handled online in Japanese via TableCheck, though booking platforms have some English support.

places.name: ザ バーキャッスル (THE BAR CASTLE)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu. Afternoon tea explicitly includes wheat-containing scones (whole wheat and hatcho miso flavored) and Atelier Ukai pastry. No dedicated GF fryer or kitchen. Standard Japanese soy sauce (wheat-containing) is likely used in savory preparations. Guests with celiac disease should contact the restaurant directly before visiting; accommodations after arrival cannot be guaranteed per their own policy.
places.vegan_detail: No dedicated vegan menu. Afternoon tea includes dairy (clotted cream, pastry), eggs, and meat-based savory items. The venue asks that guests with dietary restrictions notify them in advance at reservation time; accommodations may not be guaranteed. Bar cocktails may include dairy or egg-based ingredients. Non-alcoholic options are available.
places.hours_raw: Daily 12:00-23:30 (L.O. 23:00). Afternoon tea: two seatings at 12:00 and 14:30, served until 17:00. Bar service: 17:00-23:30 (L.O. 23:00). After 17:00, venue is restricted to guests aged 20 and over. Reservations accepted for afternoon tea; bar time walk-in only (no reservations accepted for bar time after 19:00).
places.neighborhood: 浅間町 (Sengen Cho) / Nishi Ward
places.notes: THE BAR CASTLE occupies the 4th floor of Espacio Nagoya Castle, a luxury hotel that opened in October 2025 adjacent to Nagoya Castle. The bar concept is a collaboration between the hotel and SG Group, the bartending empire built by Shingo Gokan — a Japanese bartender who moved to

roles: Head bartender program: SG Group / Shingo Gokan
Pastry / afternoon tea: Shigeo Suzuki, Atelier Ukai
chef_bio.origin: Nagoya, Aichi Prefecture (opened October 2025 as part of Espacio Nagoya Castle)
chef_bio.background: THE BAR CASTLE occupies the 4th floor of Espacio Nagoya Castle, a luxury hotel that opened in October 2025 adjacent to Nagoya Castle. The bar is a collaboration between the hotel and SG Group, the bartending group built by Shingo Gokan — a Japanese bartender who moved to New York at age 23 without speaking English, was turned away from a pastry job, and pivoted to bartending. He spent seven years at the legendary Angel's Share, won the Bacardi Legacy Cocktail Competition in 2012, and in 2018 opened The SG Club in Tokyo's Shibuya, which ranked 10th globally on The World's 50 Best Bars in 2020 and was named Best Bar in Japan. The SG Club has held a place on The World's 50 Best Bars for seven consecutive years — an achievement almost no venue in Asia can claim.
chef_bio.philosophy: Gokan's approach — 'no flash for the sake of flash, no ego for the sake of ego' — channels Japanese hospitality (omotenashi) into world-class mixology. THE BAR CASTLE adds cocktails 'with a Japanese spirit of hospitality' (和のおもてなしの心を添えたカクテル), with non-alcoholic options for sober-curious guests. The afternoon tea layers on pastry chef Shigeo Suzuki of Atelier Ukai, drawing on Japanese tea-culture aesthetics.
chef_bio.specialty: Signature cocktails from The SG Club, blending Japanese bartending sensibility with international craft; afternoon tea by Shigeo Suzuki of Atelier Ukai, featuring sweets made from Tokai-region seasonal ingredients.

anecdotes.text: Shingo Gokan moved to New York at age 23 without speaking English, was rejected for a pastry position, then pivoted to bartending. Seven years at Angel's Share sharpened his craft; he won the Bacardi Legacy Cocktail Competition in 2012 and built one of Asia's most decorated bar groups.

The SG Club reached the Top 10 of The World's 50 Best Bars — 'an achievement almost no venue in Asia can claim' — and has held a place on the list for seven consecutive years, with SG Group bars spanning Tokyo, Shanghai, and beyond.
THE BAR CASTLE features Moroccan-lamp lighting, counter seating, and an outdoor terrace overlooking the illuminated Nagoya Castle — bringing SG Group's immersive, multi-concept atmosphere to Nagoya.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The World's 50 Best・Haute Living・jouhou.nagoyaによると、後閑信吾は23歳で英語も話せずNYへ渡り、パティシエを断られてバーテンダーに転身、Angel's Shareで7年、2012年バカルディ・レガシー優勝。2018年に東京・渋谷のThe SG Clubを開業、2020年に世界のベストバー50で10位・ベストバー・イン・ジャパン。同店は世界ベストバー50に7年連続ランクイン。名古屋のTHE BAR CASTLEはEspacio Nagoya Castle 4階(2025年10月開業)で、SGグループ監修のカクテルと、鈴木滋夫(アトリエうかい)のアフタヌーンティー、モロッコランプや名古屋城を望むテラスが特徴。

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information available on dedicated GF prep areas or cross-contamination controls. The kitchen produces wheat-containing pastry (scones, Atelier Ukai sweets) in the same kitchen context. Celiac diners should not assume safety.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Savory afternoon tea items (e.g., marinated tomatoes, soup, prosciutto preparations) are likely prepared with standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat. No wheat-free soy sauce (tamari) is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Afternoon tea is built around dairy-based pastries, clotted cream, and likely egg-containing sweets. No vegan-separated prep confirmed.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The venue asks guests to notify them of allergies or ingredients they cannot consume at time of reservation. They note they may be unable to accommodate requests made after arrival, and some requests may be declined prioritizing customer safety.

positives.text: As part of a luxury international hotel, staff are accustomed to dietary-restriction conversations and communicate in English. Advance notice requests are built into their booking process via TableCheck and phone reservations.
Non-alcoholic cocktail alternatives are available for all bar items, and alcohol-free accommodations for dietary or lifestyle reasons are explicitly noted.
